abstract: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive (AR) LLMs for text generation, with the potential to decode multiple tokens in a single iteration. However, none of the existing open-source dLLMs have achieved superior inference speed over AR LLMs of similar size. This paper breaks this barrier based on a simple and effective strategy named discrete diffusion forcing (D2F). D2F equips dLLMs with two key capabilities: (1) block-wise autoregressive generation to enable KV cache utilization; (2) prediction of following tokens without requiring completion of prior blocks for inter-block parallel decoding. In this way, the vanilla dLLMs are refurbished into an AR-diffusion hybrid paradigm for efficient inference. D2F can be implemented with an asymmetric distillation process based on pre-trained dLLMs. We further propose a pipelined parallel decoding algorithm, which enables a trade-off between efficiency and efficacy. Empirically, D2F dLLMs achieve more than $\mathbf{2.5\times}$ inference speed than LLaMA3 and Qwen2.5 on GSM8K. Compared to vanilla dLLMs like LLaDA and Dream, the acceleration can be more than $\mathbf{50\times}$ while maintaining comparable output quality. The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Integrating block-wise causal attention with inter-block parallel decoding enables dLLMs to leverage efficient Key-Value (KV) cache mechanisms while maintaining high parallelism.
Knowledge distillation from a standard dLLM to a causally-restricted D2F model is an effective strategy for training complex hybrid architectures.

A specialized pipelined decoding algorithm, incorporating dynamic block management and dual-state activation, is necessary to fully realize the inference speed potential of hybrid AR-diffusion models.
originalProblem: Autoregressive (AR) Large Language Models face an inherent inference latency bottleneck due to their token-by-token sequential generation process.
Existing open-source Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have not successfully achieved faster-than-AR inference speeds while maintaining competitive output quality.
Prior dLLM acceleration techniques struggle with the incompatibility of bidirectional attention with standard Key-Value (KV) cache mechanisms or incur overhead from auxiliary models.
results: D2F-Dream-Base-7B achieved a throughput of 119.9 tokens/second on GSM8K, representing a 2.5 × speedup over LLaMA3-Instruct-8B while preserving output quality.
The method demonstrated over 50 × acceleration compared to vanilla dLLM baselines (e.g., LLaDA-Instruct-8B) with only minor performance degradation.
D2F consistently outperformed other state-of-the-art dLLM acceleration techniques, achieving up to a 2.1 × speedup over Fast-dLLM on the MATH benchmark.
Ablation studies confirmed that both the pipelined parallel decoding and the D2F training strategy are crucial for the observed performance and speed gains.
solution: Discrete Diffusion Forcing (D2F), an AR-diffusion hybrid paradigm, combines block-wise autoregressive generation with inter-block parallel decoding.

The D2F model architecture employs block-wise causal attention to enable exact Key-Value (KV) cache reuse, while allowing parallel prediction of subsequent blocks based on partially denoised prefixes.
An asymmetric distillation process trains the D2F model by transferring knowledge from a pre-trained bidirectional dLLM (teacher) to the causally restricted D2F student model.
A pipelined parallel decoding algorithm dynamically manages block generation, using a dual-state mechanism (semi-activated and fully-activated blocks) to balance efficiency and output quality.
paper_summary.summary: Discrete Diffusion Forcing (D2F) accelerates open-source Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) beyond the inference speeds of similarly-sized autoregressive (AR) LLMs by combining block-wise autoregressive generation with inter-block parallel decoding. This approach enables a 2.5 × speedup over LLaMA3-Instruct-8B on the GSM8K benchmark while preserving output quality.

title: Diffusion LLMs Can Do Faster-Than-AR Inference via Discrete Diffusion Forcing

abstract: Diffusion-based large language models (Diffusion LLMs) have shown promise for non-autoregressive text generation with parallel decoding capabilities. However, the practical inference speed of open-sourced Diffusion LLMs often lags behind autoregressive models due to the lack of Key-Value (KV) Cache and quality degradation when decoding multiple tokens simultaneously. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel block-wise approximate KV Cache mechanism tailored for bidirectional diffusion models, enabling cache reuse with negligible performance drop. Additionally, we identify the root cause of generation quality degradation in parallel decoding as the disruption of token dependencies under the conditional independence assumption. To address this, we propose a confidence-aware parallel decoding strategy that selectively decodes tokens exceeding a confidence threshold, mitigating dependency violations and maintaining generation quality. Experimental results on LLaDA and Dream models across multiple LLM benchmarks demonstrate up to \textbf{27.6$\times$ throughput} improvement with minimal accuracy loss, closing the performance gap with autoregressive models and paving the way for practical deployment of Diffusion LLMs.

keyInsights: KV activations exhibit high similarity across adjacent inference steps within a block, enabling effective approximate caching for bidirectional attention models.

Greedy parallel decoding can yield the same optimal sequence as greedy sequential decoding when token confidences are sufficiently high, providing a theoretical basis for confidence-aware parallel generation.
Dynamically adjusting the degree of parallelism based on token confidence mitigates the "curse of parallel decoding" without requiring complex auxiliary models.
originalProblem: Diffusion-based Large Language Models (LLMs) lack a standard Key-Value (KV) cache, leading to redundant computations due to their bidirectional attention mechanism.
Direct parallel decoding in Diffusion LLMs often causes a drop in generation quality due to the "conditional independence assumption" in sampling procedures.
Open-sourced Diffusion LLMs struggle to achieve the high inference speeds implied by their theoretical non-autoregressive nature, unlike highly optimized autoregressive models.
results: Achieves up to 27.6x throughput improvement on Diffusion LLMs (e.g., LLaDA on GSM8K) compared to vanilla baselines.
Maintains generation quality with negligible accuracy loss (within 1-2 percentage points) across all evaluated text-only and multimodal benchmarks.

The combination of KV Cache and parallel decoding provides the highest speedups, with KV Cache alone contributing 2x-3.6x and parallel decoding adding further acceleration, extending benefits to multimodal tasks with up to 9.9x speedup for LLaDA-V.
solution: Introduces a block-wise approximate Key-Value (KV) Cache adapted for the bidirectional attention of Diffusion LLMs, reusing activations to reduce redundant computations.

Proposes a confidence-aware parallel decoding mechanism that dynamically selects tokens for unmasking based on their predicted confidence scores, improving generation quality during parallel inference.
The entire framework is designed to be training-free, allowing direct application to existing pre-trained Diffusion LLMs for immediate acceleration.
paper_summary.summary: Fast-dLLM introduces training-free methods to accelerate Diffusion-based Large Language Models by enabling KV cache and confidence-aware parallel decoding. The approach achieves up to 27.6x throughput improvement on benchmarks like GSM8K and MBPP, while maintaining generation quality, making Diffusion LLMs more competitive for practical deployment.

title: Fast-dLLM: Training-free Acceleration of Diffusion LLM by Enabling KV Cache and Parallel Decoding

abstract:
Large language models such as GPT and Llama are trained with a next-token
prediction loss. In this work, we suggest that training language models to
predict multiple future tokens at once results in higher sample efficiency.
More specifically, at each position in the training corpus, we ask the model to
predict the following n tokens using n independent output heads, operating on
top of a shared model trunk. Considering multi-token prediction as an auxiliary
training task, we measure improved downstream capabilities with no overhead in
training time for both code and natural language models. The method is
increasingly useful for larger model sizes, and keeps its appeal when training
for multiple epochs. Gains are especially pronounced on generative benchmarks
like coding, where our models consistently outperform strong baselines by
several percentage points. Our 13B parameter models solves 12 % more problems
on HumanEval and 17 % more on MBPP than comparable next-token models.
Experiments on small algorithmic tasks demonstrate that multi-token prediction
is favorable for the development of induction heads and algorithmic reasoning
capabilities. As an additional benefit, models trained with 4-token prediction
are up to 3 times faster at inference, even with large batch sizes.

keyInsights: Predicting multiple tokens encourages LLMs to develop a more coherent understanding of language and how sequences of tokens relate.
A shared transformer trunk learns representations that are robust and capable for predicting multiple future tokens, leading to improved model performance.
A memory-efficient implementation makes training with multiple prediction heads practical for large-scale models.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) are traditionally trained using a next-token prediction paradigm, which may not be the most efficient for acquiring complex language and reasoning capabilities.
Training with only next-token prediction can limit how effectively models learn from data (sample efficiency).
Inference for LLMs can be slow as it typically generates one token at a time.
results: Models trained with multi-token prediction achieved improvements of up to +4.5 percentage points on MBPP Pass@1 and +7.5 percentage points on HumanEval Pass@100 for code generation.
The approach improved sample efficiency, allowing models to achieve better performance with fewer training tokens.
Inference speed was accelerated by up to 3 times through self-speculative decoding without sacrificing generation quality.
solution: Researchers from FAIR at Meta and affiliated institutions propose training LLMs to predict multiple future tokens simultaneously.
This is implemented by adding multiple independent prediction heads to a shared transformer trunk.
The model is trained to minimize the combined loss across all prediction heads, forcing it to anticipate a sequence of future tokens.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at FAIR at Meta and affiliated institutions introduced a method to train large language models by predicting multiple future tokens simultaneously, rather than just the immediate next one. This approach improves model performance, especially in code generation tasks with gains up to +7.5 percentage points on HumanEval, enhances reasoning capabilities, and accelerates inference speed by up to 3 times without requiring core architectural changes.

title: Better & Faster Large Language Models via Multi-token Prediction

abstract: Autoregressive language models are constrained by their inherently sequential nature, generating one token at a time. This paradigm limits inference speed and parallelism, especially during later stages of generation when the direction and semantics of text are relatively certain. In this work, we propose a novel framework that leverages the inherent knowledge of vanilla autoregressive language models about future tokens, combining techniques to realize this potential and enable simultaneous prediction of multiple subsequent tokens. Our approach introduces several key innovations: (1) a masked-input formulation where multiple future tokens are jointly predicted from a common prefix; (2) a gated LoRA formulation that preserves the original LLM's functionality, while equipping it for multi-token prediction; (3) a lightweight, learnable sampler module that generates coherent sequences from the predicted future tokens; (4) a set of auxiliary training losses, including a consistency loss, to enhance the coherence and accuracy of jointly generated tokens; and (5) a speculative generation strategy that expands tokens quadratically in the future while maintaining high fidelity. Our method achieves significant speedups through supervised fine-tuning on pretrained models. For example, it generates code and math nearly 5x faster, and improves general chat and knowledge tasks by almost 2.5x. These gains come without any loss in quality.

keyInsights: Existing autoregressive LLMs implicitly contain knowledge about future tokens, which can be explicitly leveraged for multi-token prediction.
It is possible to adapt pretrained LLMs for new capabilities like multi-token prediction without degrading their original performance by using gated adaptation mechanisms such as Gated LoRA.

Carefully designed decoding strategies, such as Quadratic Decoding, combined with coherence-enhancing components like a sampler head and Latent Consistency Matching loss, are crucial for maximizing the efficiency and reliability of multi-token generation.
originalProblem: High latency and computational cost of autoregressive LLM inference due to its inherent sequential, token-by-token generation process.
Difficulty in efficiently adapting existing powerful pretrained autoregressive LLMs for multi-token generation without degrading their original next-token prediction capabilities.
The need for practical, efficient methods to unlock and utilize the implicit future token knowledge already encoded within pretrained autoregressive models.
results: The framework achieved significant inference speedups, with acceptance rates translating to nearly 5.0x to 5.35x for code and math tasks, and approximately 2.5x for general chat and knowledge tasks, averaging 3.17x across benchmarks.
Gated LoRA successfully preserved the original model's next-token prediction (NTP) accuracy and cross-entropy loss, demonstrating no degradation of baseline performance.

Ablation studies confirmed that Quadratic Decoding, the lightweight sampler head, and Latent Consistency Matching (LCM) loss each contribute to improved speedup and acceptance rates, with their combined use showing the strongest performance.
solution: A masked-input formulation using special mask tokens appended to the input, enabling the LLM to predict multiple future tokens in parallel within a single inference step.
Gated LoRA, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that applies low-rank adaptations only when processing mask tokens, preserving the original model's next-token prediction capabilities.

A lightweight, learnable sampler module and Latent Consistency Matching (LCM) loss used during training to improve the coherence and alignment of multi-token predictions with ground-truth autoregressive outputs, enhancing speculative decoding acceptance rates.
paper_summary.summary: A framework developed by researchers at Apple enables pretrained autoregressive large language models to perform multi-token prediction, yielding inference speedups of up to 5.35x for code and math tasks and approximately 2.5x for general tasks while preserving generation quality.

title: Your LLM Knows the Future: Uncovering Its Multi-Token Prediction Potential

abstract:
Inference from large autoregressive models like Transformers is slow -
decoding K tokens takes K serial runs of the model. In this work we introduce
speculative decoding - an algorithm to sample from autoregressive models faster
without any changes to the outputs, by computing several tokens in parallel. At
the heart of our approach lie the observations that (1) hard language-modeling
tasks often include easier subtasks that can be approximated well by more
efficient models, and (2) using speculative execution and a novel sampling
method, we can make exact decoding from the large models faster, by running
them in parallel on the outputs of the approximation models, potentially
generating several tokens concurrently, and without changing the distribution.
Our method can accelerate existing off-the-shelf models without retraining or
architecture changes. We demonstrate it on T5-XXL and show a 2X-3X acceleration
compared to the standard T5X implementation, with identical outputs.

keyInsights: Speculative execution, a technique from computer architecture, can be effectively generalized to stochastic language model generation to enable parallel verification of speculative token guesses.
Even small or simple approximation models (M_q), including n-gram models, can provide meaningful acceleration by achieving non-trivial token acceptance rates.
The method increases concurrency and can reduce memory bandwidth requirements for fetching weights and KV cache, addressing common bottlenecks in large model inference.
originalProblem: Large autoregressive models, such as Transformers, suffer from high inference latency because each token must be generated sequentially, requiring multiple serial passes through the model.
Existing acceleration methods often require costly retraining, significant architectural modifications, or compromise the original model's output quality and statistical properties.
Inference from very large models is frequently bottlenecked by memory bandwidth and communication rather than raw arithmetic operations, leading to underutilized computational resources.
results: Achieved walltime speedups of 2.3X to 3.4X for Google's T5-XXL (11B parameters) on English-German translation and news summarization tasks using a T5-small (77M) approximation model.
Demonstrated token acceptance rates (α) between 0.5 and 0.9 for approximation models that were approximately two orders of magnitude smaller than the target model.
Showed that even simple, low-cost unigram and bigram models could provide speedups of up to 1.25X, highlighting the utility of minimal approximation.
solution: Introduces Speculative Decoding, which uses a smaller, faster 'approximation model' (M_q) to generate a sequence of future tokens speculatively.
The larger 'target model' (M_p) then runs in parallel on the original prefix and all subsequent prefixes to verify the speculative tokens.
Employs a novel 'Speculative Sampling' method to accept or reject the speculative tokens, rigorously ensuring that the final output distribution is identical to what M_p would produce alone.
paper_summary.summary: Google Research developed Speculative Decoding to accelerate large Transformer model inference without altering the output distribution or requiring model retraining. The method uses a smaller model to speculatively guess future tokens, which are then verified in parallel by the larger model, achieving 2X-3X walltime speedups on tasks like translation and summarization.

title: Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding

abstract: Autoregressive large language models (LLMs) deliver strong performance but require inherently sequential decoding, leading to high inference latency and poor GPU utilization. Speculative decoding mitigates this bottleneck by using a fast draft model whose outputs are verified in parallel by the target LLM; however, existing methods still rely on autoregressive drafting, which remains sequential and limits practical speedups. Diffusion LLMs offer a promising alternative by enabling parallel generation, but current diffusion models typically underperform compared with autoregressive models. In this paper, we introduce DFlash, a speculative decoding framework that employs a lightweight block diffusion model for parallel drafting. By generating draft tokens in a single forward pass and conditioning the draft model on context features extracted from the target model, DFlash enables efficient drafting with high-quality outputs and higher acceptance rates. Experiments show that DFlash achieves over 6x lossless acceleration across a range of models and tasks, delivering up to 2.5x higher speedup than the state-of-the-art speculative decoding method EAGLE-3.

keyInsights: Parallel block diffusion, when conditioned effectively, can serve as an exceptionally fast and accurate drafter, overcoming the sequential bottleneck of autoregressive speculative decoding.
Deep contextual conditioning from the target LLM, through KV injection, is critical for enabling a lightweight diffusion drafter to produce high-quality drafts with long acceptance lengths.
Tailored training strategies that mirror inference behavior and emphasize critical positions within drafted blocks significantly enhance the performance and convergence of diffusion-based drafters.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) suffer from high inference latency due to their sequential, token-by-token generation, leading to inefficient GPU utilization.
Current speculative decoding methods rely on autoregressive draft models, which introduce their own sequential bottlenecks, limiting practical speedups to approximately 2-3x.

Diffusion Language Models (dLLMs) offer parallel generation but often lack the output quality of autoregressive models and require many denoising steps, making them impractical for standalone high-quality generation.
results: DFlash achieves over 6x lossless acceleration on specific tasks (e.g., MATH-500) and an average speedup of 4.1x-4.9x across various benchmarks, surpassing state-of-the-art EAGLE-3 by up to 2.5x.
The framework consistently achieves significantly longer average acceptance lengths (e.g., ~6.5 tokens vs. ~3.4 for EAGLE-3), demonstrating superior draft quality.
Practical utility is confirmed through integration with SGLang, delivering consistent speedups (up to 5.1x on Qwen3-8B) across varying concurrency levels and model sizes on B200 GPUs.
solution: DFlash employs a lightweight block diffusion model for parallel token drafting, predicting entire blocks of future tokens in a single forward pass.
A novel KV injection strategy conditions the diffusion drafter by directly injecting fused hidden features from multiple layers of the target LLM into the drafter's Key and Value projections.

The draft model is trained with randomized masked block sampling, efficient long-context techniques, and an exponentially decaying loss weight to prioritize early token accuracy and align with speculative decoding.
paper_summary.summary: A speculative decoding framework utilizing a lightweight block diffusion model for parallel token drafting demonstrates over 6x lossless acceleration for large language model inference. This approach achieves up to 2.5x higher speedup than existing state-of-the-art methods by conditioning the drafter on target model context features.

title: DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding

abstract: Autoregressive (AR) large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable performance across a wide range of natural language tasks, yet their inherent sequential decoding limits inference efficiency. In this work, we propose Fast-dLLM v2, a carefully designed block diffusion language model (dLLM) that efficiently adapts pretrained AR models into dLLMs for parallel text generation, requiring only approximately 1B tokens of fine-tuning. This represents a 500x reduction in training data compared to full-attention diffusion LLMs such as Dream (580B tokens), while preserving the original model's performance. Our approach introduces a novel training recipe that combines a block diffusion mechanism with a complementary attention mask, enabling blockwise bidirectional context modeling without sacrificing AR training objectives. To further accelerate decoding, we design a hierarchical caching mechanism: a block-level cache that stores historical context representations across blocks, and a sub-block cache that enables efficient parallel generation within partially decoded blocks. Coupled with our parallel decoding pipeline, Fast-dLLM v2 achieves up to 2.5x speedup over standard AR decoding without compromising generation quality. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that Fast-dLLM v2 matches or surpasses AR baselines in accuracy, while delivering state-of-the-art efficiency among dLLMs - marking a significant step toward the practical deployment of fast and accurate LLMs. Code and model will be publicly released.

keyInsights: The proposed training recipe, including proper padding and a complementary masking strategy, is critical for data-efficient adaptation of AR models to a block diffusion framework, ensuring comprehensive token supervision.
A hierarchical caching approach, combining block-level and sub-block caching, effectively maximizes KV cache reuse and significantly accelerates token generation both across and within blocks.

Maintaining structural consistency between training and inference block configurations is vital for performance, a challenge addressed by the sub-block decoding strategy that offers flexible inference granularity without degradation.
originalProblem: Autoregressive (AR) LLMs face high inference latency due to their sequential, token-by-token generation, limiting real-time applications.

Existing diffusion-based LLMs (dLLMs) struggle with practical deployment due to inefficient Key-Value (KV) cache usage, high training data demands (e.g., 580 billion tokens), and often limited actual latency improvements.
Prior block diffusion models lacked comprehensive validation and scalability demonstrations on large, state-of-the-art LLMs and complex, real-world tasks.
results: Achieves up to 2.5x inference speedup over standard autoregressive decoding (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) for a 7B parameter model without sacrificing generation quality.
Reduces fine-tuning data requirements by 500x, adapting 7B models with approximately 1.31 billion tokens compared to 580 billion tokens needed by full-attention diffusion LLMs like Dream.
Matches or surpasses strong autoregressive baselines in generation quality across diverse benchmarks, with the 7B model achieving an average score of 60.3, outperforming Qwen2.5-7B-Nemo-FT's 59.6.
solution: A data-efficient training recipe adapts pretrained AR LLMs to block diffusion models using significantly reduced fine-tuning data by employing block-wise organization and a complementary masking strategy.
A hierarchical inference pipeline combines block-level autoregressive decoding with intra-block parallel refinement, utilizing a confidence-aware decoding strategy.
The DualCache mechanism is integrated to optimize sub-block computation, enabling efficient reuse of KV caches for prefix and suffix contexts during iterative refinement.
paper_summary.summary: FAST-DLLM V2 transforms pretrained autoregressive Large Language Models into efficient block-diffusion models, achieving up to 2.5x inference speedup over standard AR decoding while preserving generation quality. This method adapts 7B parameter models with approximately 1.31 billion tokens, representing a 500x reduction in fine-tuning data compared to prior diffusion LLMs.

title: Fast-dLLM v2: Efficient Block-Diffusion LLM

abstract: Autoregressive next token prediction language models offer powerful capabilities but face significant challenges in practical deployment due to the high computational and memory costs of inference, particularly during the decoding stage. We introduce Set Block Decoding (SBD), a simple and flexible paradigm that accelerates generation by integrating standard next token prediction (NTP) and masked token prediction (MATP) within a single architecture. SBD allows the model to sample multiple, not necessarily consecutive, future tokens in parallel, a key distinction from previous acceleration methods. This flexibility allows the use of advanced solvers from the discrete diffusion literature, offering significant speedups without sacrificing accuracy. SBD requires no architectural changes or extra training hyperparameters, maintains compatibility with exact KV-caching, and can be implemented by fine-tuning existing next token prediction models. By fine-tuning Llama-3.1 8B and Qwen-3 8B, we demonstrate that SBD enables a 3-5x reduction in the number of forward passes required for generation while achieving same performance as equivalent NTP training.

keyInsights: A single transformer architecture can be trained with a dual-loss objective to effectively support both autoregressive and masked token prediction modes, enabling flexible parallel decoding.

Leveraging advanced sampling algorithms, such as the Entropy Bounded Sampler from discrete diffusion literature, can provide efficient and adaptive token unmasking strategies for block-based generation.

The careful integration of masked block prediction ensures compatibility with standard KV-caching mechanisms, preserving a critical efficiency component often lost in purely diffusion-based generation.
originalProblem: The high computational and memory costs of autoregressive (NTP) language model inference, where tokens are generated one by one, leading to a decoding bottleneck.
Existing acceleration methods often introduce architectural complexity, require auxiliary draft models, or struggle with maintaining Key-Value (KV) caching efficiency.
The need for a flexible, efficient, and simple method to accelerate LLM inference without compromising model accuracy or requiring significant architectural changes.
results: SBD consistently reduced the number of forward passes (NFEs) by 3-5x on Llama-3.1 8B and Qwen-3 8B models across reasoning, coding, and mathematics benchmarks (e.g., 3.23x on MATH500, 4.59x on LiveCodeBench v6).
The method preserved equivalent performance to NTP baselines for lower speed-accuracy trade-off settings, demonstrating no degradation in model accuracy.
Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of the NTP loss term for maintaining model capabilities and validated SBD's effective integration into supervised fine-tuning (SFT) workflows.
solution: Set Block Decoding (SBD) fine-tunes existing transformer models with a dual-component loss combining standard NTP loss and Masked Token Prediction (MATP) loss on future token blocks.
During inference, SBD employs the Entropy Bounded (EB) Sampler to iteratively unmask and predict multiple, non-consecutive tokens within a block in parallel.
The approach allows for adaptive control over the speed-accuracy trade-off and maintains full compatibility with exact Key-Value (KV) caching for efficient long-context generation.
paper_summary.summary: FAIR at Meta introduces Set Block Decoding (SBD), an inference acceleration method for large language models that integrates Next Token Prediction (NTP) and Masked Token Prediction (MATP) within a single, existing transformer architecture. SBD achieves a 3-5x reduction in forward passes across Llama-3.1 8B and Qwen-3 8B models on various benchmarks while preserving performance compared to NTP baselines.

title: Set Block Decoding is a Language Model Inference Accelerator

abstract: Autoregressive decoding in language models is inherently slow, generating only one token per forward pass. We propose Parallel Token Prediction (PTP), a general-purpose framework for predicting multiple tokens in a single model call. PTP moves the source of randomness from post-hoc sampling to random input variables, making future tokens deterministic functions of those inputs and thus jointly predictable in a single forward pass. We prove that a single PTP call can represent arbitrary dependencies between tokens. PTP is trained by distilling an existing model or through inverse autoregressive training without a teacher. Experimentally, PTP achieves a 2.4x speedup on a diverse-task speculative decoding benchmark. We provide code and checkpoints at this https URL.
title: Parallel Token Prediction for Language Models

abstract:
Autoregressive decoding makes the inference of Large Language Models (LLMs)
time-consuming. In this paper, we reconsider speculative sampling and derive
two key observations. Firstly, autoregression at the feature
(second-to-top-layer) level is more straightforward than at the token level.
Secondly, the inherent uncertainty in feature (second-to-top-layer) level
autoregression constrains its performance. Based on these insights, we
introduce EAGLE (Extrapolation Algorithm for Greater Language-model
Efficiency), a simple yet highly efficient speculative sampling framework. By
incorporating a token sequence advanced by one time step, EAGLE effectively
resolves the uncertainty, enabling precise second-to-top-layer feature
prediction with minimal overhead. We conducted comprehensive evaluations of
EAGLE, including all models from the Vicuna and LLaMA2-Chat series, the MoE
model Mixtral 8x7B Instruct, and tasks in dialogue, code generation,
mathematical reasoning, and instruction following. For LLaMA2-Chat 70B, EAGLE
achieved a latency speedup ratio of 2.7x-3.5x, doubled throughput, while
maintaining the distribution of the generated text.

keyInsights: Autoregression is simpler and more regular when applied to the second-to-top-layer features of an LLM compared to direct token prediction.
Stochastic token sampling introduces significant uncertainty into subsequent feature sequences, which must be explicitly addressed by a draft model for accurate prediction.
A lightweight draft model can be effectively trained to predict next features by using a hybrid input of current features and the next sampled token, combined with a tree attention mechanism.
originalProblem: Large Language Models incur high inference latency and computational costs due to their autoregressive decoding nature.
Existing speculative sampling methods struggle to find or efficiently train suitable draft models, especially for smaller target LLMs or diverse instruction templates.
Prior methods like Medusa face low draft accuracy due to uncertainty in predicting features/tokens caused by the stochastic nature of token sampling.
results: EAGLE achieved 2.7x-3.5x latency speedup for LLaMA2-Chat 70B inference and a 2x increase in throughput compared to vanilla autoregressive decoding.
It demonstrated 1.7x-2.1x faster performance than Lookahead and 1.5x-1.6x faster than Medusa on MT-bench, while maintaining an average acceptance length of 3.2-4.5 tokens per forward pass.
The framework guarantees preservation of the target LLM's output distribution across both greedy and non-greedy decoding settings.
solution: EAGLE employs a novel drafting phase that predicts the next feature (second-to-top-layer hidden state) using a lightweight Autoregression Head.
It addresses feature uncertainty by feeding the draft model both the current feature sequence and a shifted token sequence (the sampled token) as input.
A tree attention mechanism is utilized for draft generation, allowing multiple candidate token sequences to be explored and verified in a single parallel pass by the target LLM.
paper_summary.summary: EAGLE, developed by researchers from Peking University, Microsoft Research, University of Waterloo, and Vector Institute, introduces a speculative sampling framework that accelerates Large Language Model inference by 2.7x-3.5x for LLaMA2-Chat 70B. The method improves draft accuracy by rethinking autoregression at the feature level and resolving uncertainty from stochastic token sampling, all while preserving the target LLM's output distribution.

title: EAGLE: Speculative Sampling Requires Rethinking Feature Uncertainty

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) employ auto-regressive decoding that requires
sequential computation, with each step reliant on the previous one's output.
This creates a bottleneck as each step necessitates moving the full model
parameters from High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) to the accelerator's cache. While
methods such as speculative decoding have been suggested to address this issue,
their implementation is impeded by the challenges associated with acquiring and
maintaining a separate draft model. In this paper, we present Medusa, an
efficient method that augments LLM inference by adding extra decoding heads to
predict multiple subsequent tokens in parallel. Using a tree-based attention
mechanism, Medusa constructs multiple candidate continuations and verifies them
simultaneously in each decoding step. By leveraging parallel processing, Medusa
substantially reduces the number of decoding steps required. We present two
levels of fine-tuning procedures for Medusa to meet the needs of different use
cases: Medusa-1: Medusa is directly fine-tuned on top of a frozen backbone LLM,
enabling lossless inference acceleration. Medusa-2: Medusa is fine-tuned
together with the backbone LLM, enabling better prediction accuracy of Medusa
heads and higher speedup but needing a special training recipe that preserves
the backbone model's capabilities.
  Moreover, we propose several extensions that improve or expand the utility of
Medusa, including a self-distillation to handle situations where no training
data is available and a typical acceptance scheme to boost the acceptance rate
while maintaining generation quality. We evaluate Medusa on models of various
sizes and training procedures. Our experiments demonstrate that Medusa-1 can
achieve over 2.2x speedup without compromising generation quality, while
Medusa-2 further improves the speedup to 2.3-3.6x.

keyInsights: Augmenting the original LLM with in-model decoding heads simplifies deployment and eliminates issues of distribution shift common with separate draft models in speculative decoding.
A two-stage fine-tuning strategy (MEDUSA-2) involving joint training and differential learning rates is crucial for maximizing speedup while preserving generation quality.

Self-distillation allows for training MEDUSA heads even when the original fine-tuning dataset is unavailable or when the model has undergone processes like Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF).
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) inference is bottlenecked by its auto-regressive, sequential nature, making each token generation step memory-bandwidth-bound.
Existing acceleration methods like speculative decoding often require acquiring or training a separate 'draft model', which is resource-intensive and complicates system integration.
Maintaining generation quality and avoiding distribution shifts are significant challenges when attempting to accelerate LLM inference, especially when altering the decoding process.
results: MEDUSA-1 consistently achieves 2.18x to 2.33x wall-time speedup, while MEDUSA-2 further improves speedup to 2.35x to 2.83x compared to baseline Huggingface implementations.
MEDUSA consistently outperforms open-source speculative decoding implementations, achieving significantly higher speedups (e.g., 2.83x for Vicuna-7B compared to 1.47-1.60x for speculative decoding).

The framework maintains generation quality across various model sizes and tasks, including those fine-tuned using self-distillation, demonstrating its viability for models without accessible training data.
solution: MEDUSA appends multiple lightweight decoding heads directly to the last hidden states of the original LLM, each predicting tokens at specific future positions.
It employs a novel "tree-structured attention mechanism" that enables parallel processing of multiple candidate continuations without increasing batch size.
The framework introduces "typical acceptance" as an alternative candidate acceptance scheme to standard rejection sampling, prioritizing efficiency while aiming to preserve generation quality.
paper_summary.summary: MEDUSA accelerates Large Language Model inference by augmenting the original model with multiple lightweight decoding heads and a tree-based attention mechanism, yielding up to 2.83x wall-time speedup across various models while maintaining generation quality.

title:
Medusa: Simple LLM Inference Acceleration Framework with Multiple
  Decoding Heads

abstract: Autoregressive (AR) generation is the standard decoding paradigm for Large Language Models (LLMs), but its token-by-token nature limits parallelism at inference time. Diffusion Language Models (DLLMs) offer parallel decoding by recovering multiple masked tokens per step; however, in practice they often fail to translate this parallelism into deployment speed gains over optimized AR engines (e.g., vLLM). A key reason is that many DLLMs rely on bidirectional attention, which breaks standard prefix KV caching and forces repeated contextualization, undermining efficiency. We propose WeDLM, a diffusion decoding framework built entirely on standard causal attention to make parallel generation prefix-cache friendly. The core idea is to let each masked position condition on all currently observed tokens while keeping a strict causal mask, achieved by Topological Reordering that moves observed tokens to the physical prefix while preserving their logical positions. Building on this property, we introduce a streaming decoding procedure that continuously commits confident tokens into a growing left-to-right prefix and maintains a fixed parallel workload, avoiding the stop-and-wait behavior common in block diffusion methods. Experiments show that WeDLM preserves the quality of strong AR backbones while delivering substantial speedups, approaching 3x on challenging reasoning benchmarks and up to 10x in low-entropy generation regimes; critically, our comparisons are against AR baselines served by vLLM under matched deployment settings, demonstrating that diffusion-style decoding can outperform an optimized AR engine in practice.

keyInsights: Practical speedups for parallel text generation heavily depend on "prefix cacheability" (p_cache), which requires immediate commitment of resolved tokens to the KV cache without recomputation.
Topological reordering allows causal attention to effectively access the entire observed context for mask recovery, resolving the conflict between parallel generation and AR model compatibility.

A streaming parallel decoding approach, which continuously commits left-to-right confident tokens, is more efficient than block-wise methods by maximizing cache utilization and avoiding pipeline stalls.
originalProblem: Autoregressive LLMs are bottlenecked by sequential, token-by-token decoding, leading to slow inference and underutilization of hardware accelerators.

Prior Diffusion Language Models (DLLMs) with parallel decoding struggled to achieve practical speedups over highly optimized AR inference systems due to incompatibility with standard KV caching mechanisms.
The challenge of adapting the parallel generation paradigm to existing, highly efficient causal attention-based AR inference infrastructure.
results: Demonstrated up to 3x end-to-end acceleration on complex reasoning tasks and over 10x speedup for low-entropy generation compared to optimized AR engines like vLLM.
Preserved or improved generation quality over strong AR base models (Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-8B), with average gains up to 3.6 points on diverse benchmarks, including reasoning and code generation.
Outperformed existing diffusion language models (LLaDA, Dream, SDAR) by 15-19 points on average across various benchmarks, showcasing its superior capabilities.
solution: Introduced "Topological Reordering" during training to enable causal attention to access all observed context, making mask recovery compatible with prefix caching.
Developed "Streaming Parallel Decoding," a dynamic inference procedure that continuously commits confident, left-to-right tokens to the KV cache and dynamically refills the decoding window.

Designed the entire framework to utilize standard causal attention, ensuring native compatibility with highly optimized AR inference engines and their existing optimizations like FlashAttention and vLLM.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from WeChat AI, Tencent, developed WeDLM, a diffusion language model that achieves up to 3x end-to-end acceleration on complex reasoning tasks and over 10x speedup on low-entropy generation. WeDLM is the first diffusion model to practically outperform optimized autoregressive inference engines like vLLM by ensuring compatibility with standard causal attention and KV caching, while also preserving or improving generation quality.

title: WeDLM: Reconciling Diffusion Language Models with Standard Causal Attention for Fast Inference

abstract: The generation speed of LLMs are bottlenecked by autoregressive decoding, where tokens are predicted sequentially one by one. Alternatively, diffusion large language models (dLLMs) theoretically allow for parallel token generation, but in practice struggle to achieve the speed of autoregressive models without significantly sacrificing quality. We therefore introduce adaptive parallel decoding (APD), a novel method that dynamically adjusts the number of tokens sampled in parallel. We achieve this by defining a multiplicative mixture between the dLLM marginal probabilities and the joint probability of sequences under a small auxiliary autoregressive model. This inverts the standard setup of speculative decoding, where the goal is to sample from a large autoregressive verifier by drafting from a smaller model. We further optimize APD by enabling KV caching and limiting the size of the masked input. Altogether, our method puts forward three tunable parameters to flexibly tradeoff throughput and quality. We show that APD provides markedly higher throughput with minimal quality degradations on downstream benchmarks.

keyInsights: Enforcing a left-to-right generation order for dLLMs can maintain or even improve generation quality, which is crucial for enabling controlled parallel token sampling.

A multiplicative mixture of a large dLLM (for parallel token proposals) and a smaller autoregressive model (for validating inter-token dependencies) effectively balances high throughput and generation quality.
Universal couplers provide an efficient mechanism for adaptive parallel token acceptance, allowing dynamic adjustment of parallelization without the computational overhead of rejection sampling.
originalProblem: Autoregressive Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from limited throughput due to their inherently sequential, token-by-token generation process, hindering real-time applications.

Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) theoretically possess parallel generation capabilities but struggle in practice to leverage this without significant degradation in output quality, often reverting to slow sequential decoding.
Existing dLLM acceleration methods either require retraining or lack a flexible speed-quality trade-off, while autoregressive model speedups are not directly transferable to dLLMs.
results: APD achieved an average of over 5 parallel tokens per iteration, maintaining generation quality very close to sequential (1 token per iteration) decoding, significantly outperforming naive parallelization.
Dream 7B configured with APD was found to be Pareto-optimal on the speed-quality frontier, surpassing the throughput of autoregressive models like Qwen 7B and even Qwen 0.5B.

The method provides three tunable parameters (Multiplicative Mixture Weight, Recompute KV Window, Maximum Masked Lookahead) enabling flexible trade-offs between generation throughput and output quality for diverse application requirements.
solution: Introduced Adaptive Parallel Decoding (APD), which fixes dLLM generation order to left-to-right and combines the dLLM's marginal probabilities with the joint probabilities of a smaller autoregressive model via a multiplicative mixture.

Employs a universal coupler (Gumbel-Softmax Trick) to dynamically determine the number of parallel tokens to accept per iteration, ensuring consistency with the combined target distribution without re-computation.
Incorporated architectural optimizations, including Key-Value (KV) caching with a configurable recompute window and a maximum masked lookahead parameter, to further enhance computational efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at UCLA developed Adaptive Parallel Decoding (APD), an algorithm that enables diffusion large language models (dLLMs) to generate text with substantially higher throughput by dynamically sampling multiple tokens in parallel. This method achieves Pareto-optimal performance against leading autoregressive models, with minimal impact on generation quality.

title: Accelerating Diffusion LLMs via Adaptive Parallel Decoding

abstract:
Autoregressive Models (ARMs) have long dominated the landscape of Large
Language Models. Recently, a new paradigm has emerged in the form of
diffusion-based Large Language Models (dLLMs), which generate text by
iteratively denoising masked segments. This approach has shown significant
advantages and potential. However, dLLMs suffer from high inference latency.
Traditional ARM acceleration techniques, such as Key-Value caching, are
incompatible with dLLMs due to their bidirectional attention mechanism. To
address this specific challenge, our work begins with a key observation that
dLLM inference involves a static prompt and a partially dynamic response, where
most tokens remain stable across adjacent denoising steps. Based on this, we
propose dLLM-Cache, a training-free adaptive caching framework that combines
long-interval prompt caching with partial response updates guided by feature
similarity. This design enables efficient reuse of intermediate computations
without compromising model performance. Extensive experiments on representative
dLLMs, including LLaDA 8B and Dream 7B, show that dLLM-Cache achieves up to 9.1
x speedup over standard inference without compromising output quality. Notably,
our method brings dLLM inference latency close to that of ARMs under many
settings. Codes are provided in the supplementary material and will be released
publicly on GitHub.

keyInsights: Input prompt tokens remain quasi-static across iterative denoising steps, leading to redundant recomputations if not cached.
Intermediate features of generated response tokens evolve sparsely, with only a small proportion undergoing substantial changes at any given denoising step.
The Value (V) vector's change effectively predicts subsequent changes in Attention Output and FFN Output, making it a reliable indicator for identifying dynamic tokens.
originalProblem: Diffusion-based Large Language Models (dLLMs) face high inference latency due to their multi-step iterative denoising process, requiring full forward passes at each step.
Traditional Key-Value (KV) caching methods, effective for Autoregressive Models (ARMs), are incompatible with dLLMs' bidirectional attention mechanism.
Existing diffusion model acceleration often applies uniform caching, failing to exploit the distinct characteristics of static input prompts and evolving responses in language generation.
results: Achieved substantial inference speedups: up to 9.1x for LLaDA 8B Base on LongBench-HotpotQA and 6.90x for Dream 7B on GSM8K, with LLaDA 8B on GPQA seeing an 8.08x speedup.
Maintained lossless or near-lossless output quality across various benchmarks, demonstrating acceleration without performance degradation.

Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of V-verify over other selection strategies and identified optimal parameters, such as an adaptive update ratio (ρ) of approximately 0.25 and cosine similarity as the superior metric.

Enabled dLLMs to achieve inference speeds competitive with, and in some cases surpassing, highly optimized Autoregressive Models (ARMs), such as LLaDA 8B with dLLM-Cache slightly outperforming LLaMA3 8B in Tokens Per Second on GSM8K.
solution: Implement a training-free, adaptive caching framework called dLLM-Cache that differentiates between prompt and response tokens.
Apply Long-Interval Prompt Caching to reuse intermediate features of static input prompts, refreshing them only at sparse intervals.
Utilize Adaptive Short-Interval Response Caching, performing full refreshes at shorter intervals and employing a "V-verify" mechanism for partial updates between refreshes.
The V-verify mechanism identifies the most dynamic response tokens based on the cosine similarity of their Value vectors, recomputing features only for a small, changing subset.
paper_summary.summary: A new adaptive caching framework from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, dLLM-Cache, significantly accelerates diffusion-based Large Language Models (dLLMs) by employing differentiated caching for static prompt features and dynamic response features. It achieves up to a 9.1x speedup in inference for models like LLaDA 8B and Dream 7B while maintaining or improving output quality.

title: dLLM-Cache: Accelerating Diffusion Large Language Models with Adaptive  Caching

abstract: Autoregressive decoding in large language models (LLMs) requires $\mathcal{O}(n)$ sequential steps for $n$ tokens, fundamentally limiting inference throughput. Recent diffusion-based LLMs (dLLMs) enable parallel token generation through iterative denoising. However, current parallel decoding strategies rely on fixed, input-agnostic heuristics (e.g., confidence thresholds), which fail to adapt to input-specific characteristics, resulting in suboptimal speed-quality trade-offs across diverse NLP tasks. In this work, we explore a more flexible and dynamic approach to parallel decoding. We propose Learning to Parallel Decode (Learn2PD), a framework that trains a lightweight and adaptive filter model to predict, for each token position, whether the current prediction matches the final output. This learned filter approximates an oracle parallel decoding strategy that unmasks tokens only when correctly predicted. Importantly, the filter model is learned in a post-training manner, requiring only a small amount of computation to optimize it (minute-level GPU time). Additionally, we introduce End-of-Text Prediction (EoTP) to detect decoding completion at the end of sequence, avoiding redundant decoding of padding tokens. Experiments on the LLaDA benchmark demonstrate that our method achieves up to 22.58$\times$ speedup without any performance drop, and up to 57.51$\times$ when combined with KV-Cache.

keyInsights: A simple, lightweight neural network can effectively learn a dynamic policy to approximate an 'oracle' parallel decoder, outperforming static heuristics.
Explicitly addressing and eliminating redundant token remasking and padding token decoding are crucial for unlocking the full parallelism potential of dLLMs.
The combined approach of learned token-level control and sequence-level termination provides substantial efficiency gains with minimal impact on generation quality.
originalProblem: Existing diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) suffer from redundant remasking of tokens already correctly predicted, preventing full parallel decoding speedup.
Current dLLMs waste significant computational effort by continuing to decode padding tokens after the actual end of generated content, especially for long sequences.
Static, input-agnostic parallel decoding heuristics in dLLMs fail to adapt dynamically, leading to suboptimal speed-quality trade-offs across tasks.
results: Achieved up to 22.58x speedup over the LLaDA baseline on benchmarks like GSM8K (Generation Length 1024) while maintaining negligible accuracy loss.
Demonstrated strong orthogonality with KV-Cache, yielding an impressive 57.51x speedup when combined with Dual Cache.
The filter model for Learn2PD requires minimal training overhead, taking only approximately 6 minutes on a T4 GPU after data collection.
solution: Learn2PD: A lightweight, post-trained two-layer MLP filter model that dynamically predicts and preserves correctly decoded tokens at each step, preventing unnecessary remasking.
EoTP: An early-stopping mechanism that detects the `[End-of-Text]` token and immediately truncates the generation, avoiding redundant computation on padding tokens.
The methods are orthogonal and compatible with existing dLLM acceleration techniques like Key-Value (KV) caching for compounded speedup.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Central Florida, Mobi.AI, and HKUST developed Learn2PD and EoTP, a learned parallel decoding policy and an end-of-text prediction mechanism, to accelerate diffusion-based Large Language Models (dLLMs). This approach achieved up to 22.58x speedup on benchmarks like GSM8K and MBPP with negligible accuracy loss, further scaling to 57.51x with KV-Cache integration.

title: Learning to Parallel: Accelerating Diffusion Large Language Models via Learnable Parallel Decoding

abstract: Autoregressive language models (ARMs) deliver strong likelihoods, but are inherently serial: they generate one token per forward pass, which limits throughput and inflates latency for long sequences. Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) parallelize across positions and thus appear promising for language generation, yet standard discrete diffusion typically needs hundreds to thousands of model evaluations to reach high quality, trading serial depth for iterative breadth. We introduce FS-DFM, Few-Step Discrete Flow-Matching. A discrete flow-matching model designed for speed without sacrificing quality. The core idea is simple: make the number of sampling steps an explicit parameter and train the model to be consistent across step budgets, so one big move lands where many small moves would. We pair this with a reliable update rule that moves probability in the right direction without overshooting, and with strong teacher guidance distilled from long-run trajectories. Together, these choices make few-step sampling stable, accurate, and easy to control. On language modeling benchmarks, FS-DFM with 8 sampling steps achieves perplexity parity with a 1,024-step discrete-flow baseline for generating 1,024 tokens using a similar-size model, delivering up to 128 times faster sampling and corresponding latency/throughput gains. Code & pretrained checkpoints: this https URL
title: FS-DFM: Fast and Accurate Long Text Generation with Few-Step Diffusion Language Models

abstract: We introduce AutoJudge, a method that accelerates large language model (LLM) inference with task-specific lossy speculative decoding. Instead of matching the original model output distribution token-by-token, we identify which of the generated tokens affect the downstream quality of the response, relaxing the distribution match guarantee so that the "unimportant" tokens can be generated faster. Our approach relies on a semi-greedy search algorithm to test which of the mismatches between target and draft models should be corrected to preserve quality and which ones may be skipped. We then train a lightweight classifier based on existing LLM embeddings to predict, at inference time, which mismatching tokens can be safely accepted without compromising the final answer quality. We evaluate the effectiveness of AutoJudge with multiple draft/target model pairs on mathematical reasoning and programming benchmarks, achieving significant speedups at the cost of a minor accuracy reduction. Notably, on GSM8k with the Llama 3.1 70B target model, our approach achieves up to $\approx2\times$ speedup over speculative decoding at the cost of $\le 1\%$ drop in accuracy. When applied to the LiveCodeBench benchmark, AutoJudge automatically detects programming-specific important tokens, accepting $\ge 25$ tokens per speculation cycle at $2\%$ drop in Pass@1. Our approach requires no human annotation and is easy to integrate with modern LLM inference frameworks.
title: AutoJudge: Judge Decoding Without Manual Annotation

abstract: Multi-token generation has emerged as a promising paradigm for accelerating transformer-based large model inference. Recent efforts primarily explore diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) for parallel decoding to reduce inference latency. To achieve AR-level generation quality, many techniques adapt AR models into dLLMs to enable parallel decoding. However, they suffer from limited speedup compared to AR models due to a pretrain-to-posttrain mismatch. Specifically, the masked data distribution in post-training deviates significantly from the real-world data distribution seen during pretraining, and dLLMs rely on bidirectional attention, which conflicts with the causal prior learned during pretraining and hinders the integration of exact KV cache reuse. To address this, we introduce Jacobi Forcing, a progressive distillation paradigm where models are trained on their own generated parallel decoding trajectories, smoothly shifting AR models into efficient parallel decoders while preserving their pretrained causal inference property. The models trained under this paradigm, Jacobi Forcing Model, achieves 3.8x wall-clock speedup on coding and math benchmarks with minimal loss in performance. Based on Jacobi Forcing Models' trajectory characteristics, we introduce multi-block decoding with rejection recycling, which enables up to 4.5x higher token acceptance count per iteration and nearly 4.0x wall-clock speedup, effectively trading additional compute for lower inference latency. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Progressive distillation using self-generated parallel decoding trajectories effectively aligns AR models for parallel generation while preserving their original causal inference properties.
A carefully designed progressive noise schedule is crucial for training models to predict reliably in noisy, multi-token contexts, enabling scaling to larger block sizes.

Efficient training can be achieved by integrating noise-aware causal attention and sequence packing, allowing simultaneous computation of consistency and autoregressive losses in a single forward pass.
originalProblem: Autoregressive (AR) decoding for Large Language Models suffers from high inference latency due to its sequential token generation, hindering real-time applications.
Adapting AR models for parallel decoding often leads to "pretrain-to-posttrain mismatch" or quality degradation, particularly in diffusion-based approaches.
Previous Jacobi decoding methods struggle to achieve consistent speedup with larger block sizes, as the number of correctly predicted tokens per iteration plateaus.
results: The Jacobi Forcing Model achieved up to 3.97x wall-clock speedup on coding (HumanEval, MBPP) and math (GSM8K, MATH) benchmarks, maintaining or slightly improving accuracy (e.g., 83.5% pass@1 on HumanEval vs. 87.8% AR baseline).
The method significantly outperformed state-of-the-art diffusion-based LLMs by 2-7x in speedup while delivering substantially higher generation quality.

Inference optimizations, including Rejection Recycling and Multi-block Decoding, further boosted speedup to 3.97x on HumanEval and 3.71x on GSM8K, leading to up to 4.5x higher token acceptance per iteration.
solution: Introduces Jacobi Forcing, a progressive distillation paradigm that trains autoregressive models on their self-generated parallel decoding trajectories to maintain causal inference properties.
Employs a progressive noise schedule and a progressive consistency loss, alongside standard AR loss, to enable robust multi-token prediction even with large block sizes and noisy contexts.

Integrates noise-aware causal attention with sequence packing to optimize training efficiency by processing noisy and clean blocks in a single forward pass, and proposes lossless inference optimizations like Rejection Recycling.
paper_summary.summary: Jacobi Forcing introduces a progressive distillation paradigm to enable fast and accurate causal parallel decoding for large language models, leveraging their pre-trained autoregressive capabilities. The method achieves up to 3.97x wall-clock speedup on coding and math benchmarks with negligible quality degradation, significantly outperforming prior parallel decoding techniques.

title: Fast and Accurate Causal Parallel Decoding using Jacobi Forcing

abstract: Existing techniques for accelerating language model inference, such as speculative decoding, require training auxiliary speculator models and building and deploying complex inference pipelines. We consider a new approach for converting a pretrained autoregressive language model from a slow single next token prediction model into a fast standalone multi-token prediction model using a simple online distillation objective. The final model retains the exact same implementation as the pretrained initial checkpoint and is deployable without the addition of any auxiliary verifier or other specialized inference code. On GSM8K, our method produces models that can decode more than $3\times$ faster on average at $<5\%$ drop in accuracy relative to single token decoding performance.

keyInsights: Self-distillation, where a pretrained LM acts as its own teacher, can effectively guide the model to learn to generate coherent joint distributions for multiple tokens in a single pass.
Using a "hard teacher" (argmax readout) and randomizing `k` values during training are crucial for robust multi-token prediction and entropy reduction without introducing distribution shifts.
The confidence of the student model's multi-token predictions strongly correlates with their quality, enabling dynamic and efficient inference strategies like ConfAdapt to balance speed and accuracy.
originalProblem: Large Language Models suffer from slow inference speeds due to their token-by-token autoregressive generation, particularly for long outputs required in tasks like Chain-of-Thought reasoning.
Existing Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) methods often produce incoherent or grammatically incorrect sequences because they typically predict independent marginal distributions for tokens.
Current acceleration techniques, such as speculative decoding, necessitate the training and deployment of additional auxiliary models and complex, specialized inference pipelines.
results: The method achieved over 3x average inference acceleration on the GSM8K benchmark with less than a 3% drop in accuracy for a Llama-3.1-8B model using the Confidence-Adaptive (ConfAdapt) decoding scheme.
ConfAdapt strategies consistently demonstrated Pareto-optimal trade-offs between generation speed and response quality, with acceleration factors reaching up to 5x under more aggressive settings.

MTP capabilities successfully transferred to various tasks, including other reasoning benchmarks (AIME25, GPQA, BBH) and instruction following, indicating robust generalization beyond the training domain.
solution: A "Student Forced" Online Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) objective trains a pretrained autoregressive LM (the student) to predict sequences of `k` tokens that a frozen version of itself (the teacher) would assign high likelihood.

The student generates a `k`-token sequence, which the teacher then evaluates autoregressively, and the student's loss minimizes the KL divergence between the teacher's likelihood and its own multi-token prediction distribution.
During inference, a Confidence-Adaptive (ConfAdapt) decoding scheme dynamically adjusts the number of tokens predicted (`k`) in each forward pass based on the student model's prediction confidence.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Maryland and collaborators introduced a self-distillation approach to convert pretrained autoregressive language models into multi-token predictors. This method achieved over 3x inference acceleration on benchmarks like GSM8K with less than a 3% drop in accuracy, effectively bypassing the need for auxiliary models or complex inference pipelines.

title: Multi-Token Prediction via Self-Distillation

abstract: Most large language models are autoregressive: they generate tokens one at a time. Discrete diffusion language models can generate multiple tokens in parallel, but sampling from them requires a denoising order: a strategy for deciding which tokens to decode at each step. Determining a good denoising order is difficult, and existing approaches use heuristics that create a steep trade-off between quality and latency. We propose planned diffusion, a system that trains the model to determine its own denoising order. Planned diffusion uses a single model that transitions between autoregressive and diffusion-based generation: first, the model autoregressively generates a plan that partitions the response into semantically independent chunks; second, the model denoises all chunks in parallel. The autoregressive plan enables the model to define the denoising order itself. On AlpacaEval, planned diffusion achieves 1.27x to 1.81x speedup over autoregressive generation with only 0.87% to 5.4% drop in win rate, establishing a new Pareto frontier for parallel generation with discrete diffusion. Additionally, planned diffusion's instruction following quality continues to improve with more finetuning compute, while the autoregressive baseline plateaus. Our implementation provides simple runtime knobs that offer tunable control over the quality-latency trade-off.
title: Planned Diffusion

abstract: As large language models (LLMs) become increasingly powerful, the sequential nature of autoregressive generation creates a fundamental throughput bottleneck that limits the practical deployment. While Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) has demonstrated remarkable benefits for model training efficiency and performance, its inherent potential for inference acceleration remains largely unexplored. This paper introduces FastMTP, a simple yet effective method that improves multi-step draft quality by aligning MTP training with its inference pattern, significantly enhancing speculative decoding performance. Our approach fine-tunes a single MTP head with position-shared weights on self-distilled data, enabling it to capture dependencies among consecutive future tokens and maintain high acceptance rates across multiple recursive draft steps. By integrating language-aware dynamic vocabulary compression into the MTP head, we further reduce computational overhead in the drafting process. Experimental results across seven diverse benchmarks demonstrate that FastMTP achieves an average of 2.03x speedup compared to standard next token prediction with lossless output quality, outperforming vanilla MTP by 82%. FastMTP requires only lightweight training and seamlessly integrates with existing inference frameworks, offering a practical and rapidly deployable solution for accelerating LLM inference.

keyInsights: Vanilla MTP implementations are ineffective for recursive multi-step prediction in speculative decoding because they are not explicitly trained to handle the accumulating dependencies for distant future tokens.

Fine-tuning a single MTP head with self-distilled data dramatically improves its ability to capture dependencies for multi-step prediction, leading to significantly higher and more sustained acceptance rates across several draft steps.

Language-aware vocabulary compression effectively reduces the computational cost of the MTP output head during drafting with only negligible impact on acceptance length, further boosting overall inference speed.
originalProblem: Large Language Models generate tokens autoregressively, causing inference time to scale linearly with output length, which is a bottleneck for practical deployment, especially with long Chain-of-Thoughts.

Existing Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) modules, designed for training efficiency, are largely underutilized for inference acceleration due to architectural complexities, high memory overhead, and poor performance in recursive multi-step prediction.
When a single MTP module is reused recursively for speculative decoding, its acceptance rates beyond the first predicted token drop drastically, limiting its effectiveness.
results: FastMTP achieves an average of 2.03× speedup over vanilla autoregressive decoding and an 82% improvement over a vanilla MTP reusing strategy, maintaining lossless output quality across diverse benchmarks.
The method demonstrates significantly improved acceptance rates for multi-step drafting, achieving 80% at k=1, 56% at k=2, and 36% at k=3, compared to less than 10% for vanilla MTP beyond k=1.

Optimal performance is observed at K=3 draft steps, reaching a peak decoding speed of 140 tokens/s on an A100 GPU, with language-aware dynamic vocabulary compression contributing a further 22% increase in speedup.
solution: A single MTP head with shared weights is trained to recursively predict K additional tokens using a weighted cross-entropy loss, with only this head (less than 3% of total parameters) fine-tuned via self-distillation.

Language-aware dynamic vocabulary compression is introduced to reduce the computational overhead of the MTP output head during drafting, by dynamically selecting a high-frequency token subset based on the inferred language.
These enhancements are integrated into an EAGLE-style speculative decoding framework, where the main model verifies the MTP-generated draft tokens in parallel, guaranteeing lossless output quality.
paper_summary.summary: Tencent researchers developed FastMTP, a method that accelerates Large Language Model inference by integrating a fine-tuned, shared-weight Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) head with language-aware dynamic vocabulary compression into a speculative decoding framework. The approach achieves an average 2.03× speedup over vanilla autoregressive decoding with lossless output quality, significantly outperforming previous MTP reuse strategies.

title: FastMTP: Accelerating LLM Inference with Enhanced Multi-Token Prediction

abstract: Speculative Decoding (SD) is a widely used approach to accelerate the inference of large language models (LLMs) without reducing generation quality. It operates by first using a compact model to draft multiple tokens efficiently, followed by parallel verification using the target LLM. This approach leads to faster inference compared to auto-regressive decoding. While there are multiple approaches to create a draft model, one promising approach is to use early-exit methods. These methods draft candidate tokens by using a subset of layers of the primary model and applying the remaining layers for verification, allowing a single model to handle both drafting and verification. While this technique reduces memory usage and computational cost, its performance relies on the choice of the exit layer for drafting and the number of tokens drafted (speculation length) in each SD round. Prior works use hyperparameter exploration to statically select these values. However, our evaluations show that these hyperparameter values are task-specific, and even within a task they are dependent on the current sequence context. We introduce DEL (Dynamic Exit Layer), a plug-and-play method that adaptively selects the exit layer and speculation length during inference. DEL dynamically tracks the token acceptance rate if the tokens are drafted at each layer of an LLM and uses that knowledge to heuristically select the optimal exit layer and speculation length. Our experiments across a broad range of models and downstream tasks show that DEL achieves overall speedups of $2.16\times$$\sim$$2.62\times$ over vanilla auto-regressive decoding and improves upon state-of-the-art SD methods, which peak at $2.43\times$, by up to $0.19\times$. The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Optimal configurations for the exit layer and speculation length in self-speculative decoding are highly dynamic and context-dependent, necessitating real-time adaptation.
Intermediate hidden states, already computed by early-exit methods like LayerSkip, can be effectively repurposed to predict token acceptance and confidence for all potential exit layers.
A cost-benefit metric like Token-per-Layer (TPL) can guide the dynamic selection of drafting parameters to maximize decoding throughput.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) auto-regressive decoding is inherently slow, causing high inference latency and inefficient hardware utilization.

Existing self-speculative decoding methods, like LayerSkip, rely on statically chosen hyperparameters (exit layer E and speculation length \gamma), leading to suboptimal performance that varies significantly across tasks, prompts, and even within a single generation sequence.
results: Achieved overall speedups of 2.16x to 2.62x compared to vanilla auto-regressive decoding across diverse LLMs (LLaMA-1B to 70B) and tasks.
Outperformed state-of-the-art speculative decoding baselines, demonstrating up to a 12% relative gain over the best baseline (FS) on LLaMA-2-7B.

Maintained output quality, producing identical sequences for greedy decoding and consistent ROUGE-2 scores for sampling, while introducing minimal runtime and memory overhead (average 1.54% memory overhead).
solution: DEL introduces a plug-and-play module that dynamically selects the optimal exit layer (E) and speculation length (\gamma) for each round of speculative decoding during inference.
It defines a novel Token-per-Layer (TPL) metric to quantify decoding efficiency, estimating the expected number of tokens generated per loaded layer.
The method efficiently estimates layer-wise acceptance rates and confidence scores using 'shadow tokens' derived from intermediate hidden states, avoiding additional forward passes.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Southern California developed DEL, a context-aware dynamic exit layer method for self-speculative decoding that adaptively adjusts drafting parameters during LLM inference. This approach achieves 2.16x to 2.62x speedup over vanilla auto-regressive decoding and outperforms existing speculative decoding baselines by up to 12% relative gain, all with minimal overhead and preserved output quality.

title: DEL: Context-Aware Dynamic Exit Layer for Efficient Self-Speculative Decoding

abstract:
Autoregressive (AR) Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated
significant success across numerous tasks. However, the AR modeling paradigm
presents certain limitations; for instance, contemporary autoregressive LLMs
are trained to generate one token at a time, which can result in noticeable
latency. Recent advances have indicated that search and repeated sampling can
enhance performance in various applications, such as theorem proving, code
generation, and alignment, by utilizing greater computational resources during
inference. In this study, we demonstrate that diffusion language models are
capable of generating at least 32 tokens simultaneously, while exceeding the
performance of AR models in text quality and on the LAMBADA natural language
understanding benchmark. This outcome is achieved through a novel distillation
method for discrete diffusion models, which reduces the number of inference
steps by a factor of 32-64. Practically, at the 1.3B parameters scale,
diffusion models, even without caching, can generate tokens at a rate that is
up to 8 times faster than AR models employing KV-caching, and we anticipate
further improvements with the inclusion of caching. Moreover, we demonstrate
the efficacy of our approach for diffusion language models with up to 860M
parameters.

keyInsights: Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KLD) consistently serves as the most effective distillation loss for discrete diffusion models, particularly the reverse KLD.
Iterated, progressive self-distillation (e.g., gradually reducing steps from 1024 to 32) is crucial for stable training and achieving large reductions in inference steps.
