SDTT enables drastic speedups for discrete diffusion models while minimally affecting sample diversity, a common challenge in knowledge distillation for generative models.
originalProblem: Autoregressive Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from high inference latency due to their sequential token generation, hindering applications requiring extensive sampling or real-time responses.

Discrete diffusion language models (DLMs), despite offering parallel generation capabilities, are impractically slow due to the thousands of denoising steps traditionally required for high-quality output.

Existing knowledge distillation techniques for continuous diffusion models often rely on deterministic mappings that are not directly applicable to the absorbing discrete diffusion process used by DLMs.
results: Self-Distilled Through Time (SDTT) enables discrete diffusion models to generate tokens up to 8 times faster than KV-cached autoregressive models (e.g., GPT-2) at the 1.3B parameter scale.

The method successfully reduced DLM inference steps by factors of 32-64 (e.g., from 1024 down to 32 or 16 steps) while achieving lower generative perplexity and improved MAUVE scores compared to the original teacher model.
Distilled models, demonstrated up to 860M parameters, show improved accuracy on the LAMBADA benchmark and strong preservation of performance on other natural language understanding tasks.
solution: The paper introduces Self-Distillation Through Time (SDTT), a novel knowledge distillation approach specifically for discrete diffusion language models.

SDTT trains a 'student' denoiser to approximate the multi-step output of a 'teacher' denoiser in a single step by collecting log probabilities from the teacher's intermediate denoising process as distillation targets.
To achieve substantial step reductions stably, SDTT is applied iteratively; the student model from one distillation round becomes the teacher for the next round.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at EPFL developed Self-Distillation Through Time (SDTT), a method that significantly accelerates discrete diffusion language models by reducing their inference steps by factors of 32-64. The approach enables these models to generate text up to 8 times faster than autoregressive models like GPT-2 while maintaining or improving text generation quality and language understanding capabilities.

title: Beyond Autoregression: Fast LLMs via Self-Distillation Through Time

abstract:
Speculative decoding has proven to be an efficient solution to large language
model (LLM) inference, where the small drafter predicts future tokens at a low
cost, and the target model is leveraged to verify them in parallel. However,
most existing works still draft tokens auto-regressively to maintain sequential
dependency in language modeling, which we consider a huge computational burden
in speculative decoding. We present ParallelSpec, an alternative to
auto-regressive drafting strategies in state-of-the-art speculative decoding
approaches. In contrast to auto-regressive drafting in the speculative stage,
we train a parallel drafter to serve as an efficient speculative model.
ParallelSpec learns to efficiently predict multiple future tokens in parallel
using a single model, and it can be integrated into any speculative decoding
framework that requires aligning the output distributions of the drafter and
the target model with minimal training cost. Experimental results show that
ParallelSpec accelerates baseline methods in latency up to 62% on text
generation benchmarks from different domains, and it achieves 2.84X overall
speedup on the Llama-2-13B model using third-party evaluation criteria.
title: ParallelSpec: Parallel Drafter for Efficient Speculative Decoding

abstract:
Decoding with autoregressive large language models (LLMs) traditionally
occurs sequentially, generating one token after another. An emerging line of
work explored parallel decoding by identifying and simultaneously generating
semantically independent chunks of LLM responses. However, these techniques
rely on hand-crafted heuristics tied to syntactic structures like lists and
paragraphs, making them rigid and imprecise. We present PASTA, a learning-based
system that teaches LLMs to identify semantic independence and express parallel
decoding opportunities in their own responses. At its core are PASTA-LANG and
its interpreter: PASTA-LANG is an annotation language that enables LLMs to
express semantic independence in their own responses; the language interpreter
acts on these annotations to orchestrate parallel decoding on-the-fly at
inference time. Through a two-stage finetuning process, we train LLMs to
generate PASTA-LANG annotations that optimize both response quality and
decoding speed. Evaluation on AlpacaEval, an instruction following benchmark,
shows that our approach Pareto-dominates existing methods in terms of decoding
speed and response quality; our results demonstrate geometric mean speedups
ranging from 1.21x to 1.93x with corresponding quality changes of +2.2% to
-7.1%, measured by length-controlled win rates against sequential decoding
baseline.

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LLMs) can be trained to autonomously identify and express semantic independence within their own generated responses, moving beyond fixed heuristic rules.

A custom annotation language (PASTA-LANG), coupled with an efficient interpreter and a two-stage finetuning process (including preference optimization), enables effective learned asynchronous decoding.
Preference optimization is crucial for aligning theoretical parallelization opportunities with realized speedup and maintaining response quality, mitigating issues like 'ineffective parallelism'.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from high inference latency due to their autoregressive, token-by-token decoding, leading to underutilization of parallel computing hardware.
Existing asynchronous decoding methods rely on brittle, hand-crafted heuristics (e.g., regular expressions) to detect parallelizable content, limiting their scalability, robustness, and precision.
Achieving significant decoding speedup often compromises response quality, posing a challenge for practical LLM deployment.
results: PASTA models consistently Pareto-dominate existing asynchronous decoding strategies (like APAR and SoT) in the quality-speedup trade-off on the AlpacaEval benchmark.
PASTA-BoN-2 achieved a 1.62x speedup with only a 5% drop in win rate against the baseline, while PASTA-BoN-1 reached a 1.93x speedup with a 42.9% win rate.
The preference optimization effectively mitigates 'ineffective parallelism' observed in initial models, successfully translating theoretical parallelism into realized speedups and preserving quality.
solution: The PASTA system introduces PASTA-LANG, an XML-like annotation language that allows LLMs to explicitly mark semantically independent content chunks for parallel decoding.
An intelligent PASTA-LANG interpreter orchestrates asynchronous decoding by initiating new threads for promised content and managing a shared, interleaved KV-cache.

A two-stage finetuning process trains LLMs: first, Supervised Finetuning (SFT) on an automatically annotated dataset, followed by preference optimization to balance speedup and quality using a combined score.
paper_summary.summary: The PASTA system, developed by researchers from MIT CSAIL and Google, enables Large Language Models to autonomously identify and exploit parallel decoding opportunities within their own outputs. This learned asynchronous decoding approach significantly reduces LLM inference latency, achieving up to 1.93x speedup while maintaining or improving response quality compared to heuristic-based methods.

title: Learning to Keep a Promise: Scaling Language Model Decoding Parallelism  with Learned Asynchronous Decoding

abstract:
Historically, LLMs have been trained using either autoregressive (AR) or
masked language modeling (MLM) objectives, with AR models gaining dominance in
recent years. However, AR models are inherently incapable of masked infilling,
which is the ability to predict masked tokens between past and future context.
In contrast, MLM models suffer from intrinsic computational inefficiencies
during both training and inference that hinder their scalability. This work
introduces MARIA (Masked and Autoregressive Infilling Architecture), a novel
approach that leverages the strengths of both paradigms to achieve
state-of-the-art masked infilling performance. MARIA combines a pre-trained MLM
and AR model by training a linear decoder that takes their concatenated hidden
states as input. This minimal modification enables the AR model to perform
infilling while retaining its inherent advantages in terms of faster inference
with KV caching. Our results demonstrate that MARIA significantly outperforms
existing methods, namely discrete diffusion models, on masked infilling tasks.

keyInsights: Concatenated hidden states from both model types provide complementary information
AR model advantages can be preserved while adding MLM capabilities
Efficient inference possible through careful architectural choices
originalProblem: Traditional autoregressive models cannot perform masked infilling tasks
Existing masked language models are computationally inefficient for infilling
Previous hybrid approaches faced significant scalability challenges
results: Superior performance on masked infilling tasks compared to existing methods
Lower perplexity across various masking rates
Better token-level representations for downstream tasks
Maintained fast inference capabilities through KV caching
Minimal parameter overhead compared to baseline models
solution: Novel architecture combining pre-trained MLM and AR models via trainable linear decoder
Product initialization technique for faster convergence
Maintained KV caching capability for efficient inference
Minimal modification to existing model architectures
paper_summary.summary: UCLA researchers introduce MARIA, a breakthrough hybrid architecture that enables autoregressive language models to perform masked token infilling without sacrificing computational efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art performance through clever integration of MLM and AR capabilities while requiring minimal additional parameters.

title: Enabling Autoregressive Models to Fill In Masked Tokens

abstract: Text generation with Large Language Models (LLMs) is known to be memory bound due to the combination of their auto-regressive nature, huge parameter counts, and limited memory bandwidths, often resulting in low token rates. Speculative decoding has been proposed as a solution for LLM inference acceleration. However, since draft models are often unavailable in the modern open-source LLM families, e.g., for Llama 2 7B, training a high-quality draft model is required to enable inference acceleration via speculative decoding. In this paper, we propose a simple draft model training framework for direct alignment to chat-capable target models. With the proposed framework, we train Llama 2 Chat Drafter 115M, a draft model for Llama 2 Chat 7B or larger, with only 1.64\% of the original size. Our training framework only consists of pretraining, distillation dataset generation, and finetuning with knowledge distillation, with no additional alignment procedure. For the finetuning step, we use instruction-response pairs generated by target model for distillation in plausible data distribution, and propose a new Total Variation Distance++ (TVD++) loss that incorporates variance reduction techniques inspired from the policy gradient method in reinforcement learning. Our empirical results show that Llama 2 Chat Drafter 115M with speculative decoding achieves up to 2.3 block efficiency and 2.4$\times$ speed-up relative to autoregressive decoding on various tasks with no further task-specific fine-tuning.

keyInsights: Minimizing the Total Variation Distance (TVD) between the draft and target model output distributions is theoretically equivalent to maximizing the acceptance rate in speculative decoding.
The gradient of TVD can be linked to a policy-gradient step in reinforcement learning, allowing the application of variance reduction techniques to create the more effective TVD++ loss.
Empirical observations confirmed that initial pretraining of the draft model on general language data is crucial for achieving better alignment with the target model.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from slow inference due to their auto-regressive nature, hindering real-time applications and increasing operational costs.
There is a lack of high-quality, ultra-small (sub-billion parameter) draft models specifically designed for modern chat-capable LLMs to effectively leverage speculative decoding.
Training effective draft models is challenging due to the frequent unavailability of the original pretraining and alignment datasets used for target LLMs.
results: The framework successfully trained "Llama 2 Chat Drafter 115M," a draft model only 1.64% the size of the Llama 2 Chat 7B target model.
Speculative decoding with the trained draft model achieved up to 2.4x speed-up in token generation rate and 2.3 block efficiency across diverse tasks without additional fine-tuning.
The TVD++ loss consistently outperformed or matched KLD and standard TVD losses, showing higher Memory-Bound Speed-Up (MBSU) (e.g., ~2.4 vs. ~2.2-2.3 on Dolly).
solution: A three-phase training pipeline was proposed, encompassing draft model pretraining, distillation dataset generation, and fine-tuning with knowledge distillation.
Distillation data was generated by having the target LLM respond to seed instructions from publicly available datasets, circumventing the need for original target model training data.

A novel Total Variation Distance++ (TVD++) loss function was introduced, drawing connections to reinforcement learning's policy gradients to directly optimize for the speculative decoding acceptance rate.
paper_summary.summary: Qualcomm AI Research developed a three-phase framework for directly aligning a 115-million-parameter draft model with chat-fine-tuned Large Language Models like Llama 2 Chat 7B for speculative decoding. This approach, which includes a novel TVD++ loss function, achieved up to 2.4x speed-up in token generation rate and 2.3 block efficiency without requiring the target model's original training data.

title: Direct Alignment of Draft Model for Speculative Decoding with Chat-Fine-Tuned LLMs

abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive large language model (LLM) inference by using a lightweight draft model to propose candidate tokens that are then verified in parallel by the target model. The speedup is significantly determined by the acceptance rate, yet standard training minimizes Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence as a proxy objective. While KL divergence and acceptance rate share the same global optimum, small draft models, having limited capacity, typically converge to suboptimal solutions where minimizing KL does not guarantee maximizing acceptance rate. To address this issue, we propose LK losses, special training objectives that directly target acceptance rate. Comprehensive experiments across four draft architectures and six target models, ranging from 8B to 685B parameters, demonstrate consistent improvements in acceptance metrics across all configurations compared to the standard KL-based training. We evaluate our approach on general, coding and math domains and report gains of up to 8-10% in average acceptance length. LK losses are easy to implement, introduce no computational overhead and can be directly integrated into any existing speculator training framework, making them a compelling alternative to the existing draft training objectives.

keyInsights: A detailed gradient analysis showed that while Total Variation (TV) distance is theoretically ideal for maximizing acceptance rate, its vanishing gradients and non-differentiability make it impractical for training draft models from random initialization.
KL divergence, while providing stable gradients at initialization, is a 'mode-covering' objective that leads to suboptimal acceptance rates when draft model capacity is limited.
An adaptive blending schedule between KL and TV (as in `LλLK`) is crucial for combining the smooth optimization of KL early on with the direct acceptance rate optimization of TV later in training.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) inference is bottlenecked by the sequential, autoregressive generation of tokens, underutilizing hardware accelerators.
The efficiency of speculative decoding, a key acceleration technique, is critically dependent on the acceptance rate of drafted tokens.
Current draft model training objectives, primarily Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, are suboptimal for capacity-limited models as they do not directly maximize the acceptance rate.
results: LK losses consistently improved the average acceptance length (τ) by up to 8-10% compared to KL divergence across various draft architectures and target models ranging from 8B to 685B parameters.

The greatest performance gains were observed for lower-capacity draft models and when targeting large Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models (e.g., +8.2% for Qwen3-235B-A22B), indicating their effectiveness in bridging capacity gaps.

The proposed methods act as a drop-in replacement for existing KL-based objectives, introducing no computational overhead during training or inference and demonstrating applicability even for fine-tuning pre-trained multi-token prediction modules.
solution: Proposed `LλLK`, a hybrid objective that adaptively blends KL divergence and Total Variation (TV) distance, using a dynamic schedule to leverage KL for early training stability and TV for direct acceptance rate optimization.

Introduced `LαLK`, a likelihood-based objective which implicitly provides an adaptive scaling factor (`1/α`) to the TV distance gradient, effectively resolving the vanishing gradient problem during early training.
Designed both LK losses to naturally handle truncated vocabularies, enabling direct optimization with respect to the original target distribution without modification.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Nebius developed new training objectives, termed 'LK losses,' for speculative decoding that directly optimize the acceptance rate of drafted tokens rather than relying on Kullback-Leibler divergence. These losses achieved consistent improvements in average acceptance length, demonstrating gains of up to 8-10% across diverse large language model architectures and scales, thereby enhancing inference efficiency.

title: LK Losses: Direct Acceptance Rate Optimization for Speculative Decoding

abstract: Speculative decoding is an effective and lossless approach for accelerating LLM inference. However, existing widely adopted model-based draft designs, such as EAGLE3, improve accuracy at the cost of multi-step autoregressive inference, resulting in high drafting latency and ultimately rendering the drafting stage itself a performance bottleneck. Inspired by diffusion-based large language models (dLLMs), we propose DART, which leverages parallel generation to reduce drafting latency. DART predicts logits for multiple future masked positions in parallel within a single forward pass based on hidden states of the target model, thereby eliminating autoregressive rollouts in the draft model while preserving a lightweight design. Based on these parallel logit predictions, we further introduce an efficient tree pruning algorithm that constructs high-quality draft token trees with N-gram-enforced semantic continuity. DART substantially reduces draft-stage overhead while preserving high draft accuracy, leading to significantly improved end-to-end decoding speed. Experimental results demonstrate that DART achieves a 2.03x--3.44x wall-clock time speedup across multiple datasets, surpassing EAGLE3 by 30% on average and offering a practical speculative decoding framework. Code is released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Parallel token prediction, inspired by diffusion models but adapted for causal LLM generation, can drastically reduce drafting latency without sacrificing output quality.

Specialized training objectives, such as annealed KL divergence with prefix-shared masked training, are crucial for effectively aligning a parallel drafter with the causal constraints of autoregressive decoding.
Integrating N-gram continuity scores into a tree pruning algorithm is highly effective for converting independently predicted parallel logits into high-quality, semantically coherent draft sequences.
The 'shifted logits prediction' scheme empirically boosts prediction accuracy, particularly for the critical first draft position, improving overall speculative decoding efficiency.
originalProblem: Existing model-based speculative decoding methods like EAGLE3 are bottlenecked by high drafting latency because their lightweight draft models still operate autoregressively.
Directly integrating existing standalone diffusion-based LLMs (dLLMs) into speculative decoding is challenging due to modeling mismatches (bidirectional vs. causal context) and high inference costs.
Heuristic-based drafting methods, while low latency, often suffer from low predictive accuracy, leading to modest overall speedups.
results: DART achieves a wall-clock time speedup of 2.03x to 3.44x over vanilla autoregressive decoding and outperforms EAGLE3 by 30% on average, establishing a new state-of-the-art.
The method dramatically reduces drafting forward latency by up to 6.8x compared to EAGLE3 and 53.3x compared to standard speculative sampling (SPS), making drafting overhead negligible (total 3.5ms).
DART maintains a high average acceptance length (τ) comparable to EAGLE3 while significantly boosting end-to-end throughput, with performance gains up to 65% over EAGLE3 on code-centric benchmarks.
solution: DART employs a lightweight, single-layer Transformer draft model that uses a diffusion-inspired mechanism to predict multiple future tokens in parallel from a single forward pass, eliminating autoregressive drafting.
A prefix-shared masked training strategy, combined with an annealed KL divergence objective, efficiently trains the parallel drafter while prioritizing accuracy for early draft positions.
An N-gram-guided tree pruning algorithm is used to efficiently construct semantically coherent draft sequences from the parallel logits for verification by the target model.
paper_summary.summary: DART introduces a novel speculative decoding framework that leverages a diffusion-inspired parallel drafting mechanism to accelerate large language model (LLM) inference. The method achieves end-to-end throughput speedups ranging from 2.03x to 3.44x over vanilla autoregressive decoding and surpasses previous state-of-the-art methods like EAGLE3 by 30% on average, primarily by reducing drafting latency up to 6.8x.

title: DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference

abstract: Autoregressive (AR) language models generate text one token at a time, which limits their inference speed. Diffusion-based language models offer a promising alternative, as they can decode multiple tokens in parallel. However, we identify a key bottleneck in current diffusion LMs: the long decoding-window problem, where tokens generated far from the input context often become irrelevant or repetitive. Previous solutions like semi-autoregressive address this issue by splitting windows into blocks (sacrificing bidirectionality), but we find that this also leads to time-interval expansion problem, sacrificing the speed. Therefore, semi-AR eliminates the main advantages of diffusion models. To overcome this, we propose Convolutional decoding (Conv), a normalization-based method that narrows the decoding window without hard segmentation, leading to better fluency and flexibility. Additionally, we introduce Rejecting Rule-based Fine-Tuning (R2FT), a post-hoc training scheme that better aligns tokens at positions far from context. Our methods achieve state-of-the-art results on open-ended generation benchmarks (e.g., AlpacaEval) among diffusion LM baselines, with significantly lower step size than previous works, demonstrating both speed and quality improvements.

keyInsights: The "long decoding-window problem" is a critical bottleneck for fluency and coherence in diffusion LMs, causing unaligned token predictions far from the input context.

Current semi-autoregressive methods for diffusion LMs compromise both the potential for high speed and bidirectional generation, failing to fully leverage the core advantages of diffusion architectures.

Preference optimization can be effectively achieved using rule-based negative samples, offering a cost-efficient alternative to human feedback for mitigating specific undesirable generation patterns like repetition.
originalProblem: Autoregressive language models face inherent limitations in inference speed due to sequential token generation and lack true bidirectionality.
Diffusion language models, despite theoretical advantages like parallel decoding, suffer from the "long decoding-window problem," which leads to irrelevant or repetitive token generation.

Existing semi-autoregressive solutions to shorten the decoding window introduce a "time-interval expansion problem" (quality degradation at low decoding steps) and sacrifice the inherent bidirectionality of diffusion models.
results: The combined Conv and R2FT approach achieved state-of-the-art performance among diffusion language models across open-ended generation benchmarks like AlpacaEval, MT-Bench, and Wiki.

The methods demonstrated a significant speed advantage, producing text approximately 3x faster than autoregressive models at highly compressed decoding step sizes (e.g., 128 steps for 1024-token generation) while maintaining quality.

Convolutional decoding proved more robust and stable across varying kernel sizes compared to semi-autoregressive methods, which experienced sharp performance degradation at reduced block sizes, validating the avoidance of the "time-interval expansion problem."
solution: Proposed Convolutional decoding (Conv), a normalization-based method that smoothly narrows the effective decoding window during inference, preserving speed and bidirectionality without rigid block segmentation.

Introduced Rejecting Rule-based Fine-Tuning (R2FT), a post-hoc training scheme that leverages synthetically generated rule-based negative samples to reduce the model's preference for repetitive or high-prior tokens.
Integrated EOS-fill, a technique that populates all subsequent positions with End-Of-Sequence tokens upon sampling, accelerating decoding by reducing the number of masked tokens.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Yonsei University developed Convolutional decoding (Conv) and Rejecting Rule-based Fine-Tuning (R2FT) to address limitations in diffusion language models, specifically the "long decoding-window problem" and "time-interval expansion problem." Their approach achieves state-of-the-art performance among diffusion LMs in open-ended text generation and generates text approximately 3x faster than autoregressive models at reduced decoding steps.

title: Fast and Fluent Diffusion Language Models via Convolutional Decoding and Rejective Fine-tuning

abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) scale up, inference efficiency becomes a critical bottleneck. Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) could accelerate LLM inference by predicting multiple future tokens in parallel. However, existing MTP approaches still face two challenges: limited acceptance rates of MTP heads, and difficulties in jointly training multiple MTP heads. Therefore, we propose MTP-D, a simple yet effective self-distillation method with minimal additional training cost, which boosts MTP head acceptance rates (+7.5\%) while maximumly preserving main-head performance. We also introduce a looped extension strategy for MTP-D, enabling effective and economical MTP head extension and further significant inference speedup to 1-head MTP (+220.4\%). Moreover, we systematically explore and validate key insights on the distillation strategies and the potential scalability of MTP through extensive experiments on seven benchmarks. These results demonstrate that our MTP-D and looped extension strategy effectively enhance MTP-head performance and inference efficiency, facilitating the practical usage of MTP in LLMs.

keyInsights: Gradient detachment is crucial for self-distillation in MTP to prevent performance degradation of the main LLM head.
Distilling over a TopN subset of logits (e.g., TopN=10,000) is more computationally efficient and stable than full-vocabulary distillation.

The cascaded MTP architecture demonstrates inherent scalability, and MTP-D further enhances this, allowing for effective extension to a higher number of MTP heads with relatively limited continued pre-training data.
originalProblem: High inference latency and computational costs for Large Language Models (LLMs) due to the sequential nature of Next-Token Prediction (NTP).
Existing Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) methods suffer from low acceptance rates of speculated tokens, which diminishes actual inference speedup.
Challenges in jointly training multiple MTP heads, including optimization difficulties and potential degradation of the main LLM head's performance, limit the practical scalability of MTP.
results: MTP-D increased the average acceptance rate by 3.6% for single MTP heads, leading to an approximate 14% inference speedup, and by 7.5% for four MTP heads, resulting in about 22.9% speedup.
The method consistently preserved or slightly improved the main LLM head's accuracy across various configurations and model sizes (2B Dense, N10BA1B MoE).
The looped extension strategy enabled MTP-D to scale effectively up to 16 MTP heads, achieving up to 107.4% speedup when comparing a 4-head MTP-D configuration to a 1-head MTP-D baseline.
solution: Introduces MTP-D, a self-distillation method that aligns MTP head logit distributions with the main head using a gradient-detached, TopN-logits-selected Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence loss during pre-training.
Applies a 'stop-gradient' operation to the main head logits during distillation, ensuring MTP head improvements without negatively impacting main-head performance.

Proposes a 'looped extension' strategy for continued pre-training, allowing for economical scaling of MTP heads by initializing new groups of heads from previously trained ones while freezing earlier components.
paper_summary.summary: A self-distillation method, MTP-D, is introduced to improve Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) acceptance rates in Large Language Models during pre-training, achieving up to 107.4% inference speedup with preserved main-head accuracy. The research also proposes a looped extension strategy to scalably increase the number of MTP heads.

title: Self-Distillation for Multi-Token Prediction

abstract: Current Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems concatenate and process numerous retrieved document chunks for prefill which requires a large volume of computation, therefore leading to significant latency in time-to-first-token (TTFT). To reduce the computation overhead as well as TTFT, we introduce TurboRAG, a novel RAG system that redesigns the inference paradigm of the current RAG system by first pre-computing and storing the key-value (KV) caches of documents offline, and then directly retrieving the saved KV cache for prefill. Hence, online computation of KV caches is eliminated during inference. In addition, we provide a number of insights into the mask matrix and positional embedding mechanisms, plus fine-tune a pretrained language model to maintain model accuracy of TurboRAG. Our approach is applicable to most existing large language models and their applications without any requirement in modification of models and inference systems. Experimental results across a suite of RAG benchmarks demonstrate that TurboRAG reduces TTFT by up to 9.4x compared to the conventional RAG systems (on an average of 8.6x), but reserving comparable performance to the standard RAG systems.

keyInsights: An "Independent Attention" mask prevents inappropriate cross-attention between different document chunks, maintaining document independence.
The "Reordered Positions" approach resolves position ID inconsistency by creating a monotonically increasing sequence across concatenated document KV caches.
Fine-tuning a pre-trained language model with instruction learning data incorporating the new attention patterns and position ID handling is essential to prevent accuracy degradation.
originalProblem: Conventional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems suffer from high inference latency due to processing retrieved documents online, particularly in the prefill phase.
The same document chunks' key-value (KV) caches are repeatedly recomputed for different queries, leading to redundant computation.
The quadratic computational cost of generating KV caches with respect to input sequence length and limited batch sizes further exacerbate latency issues.
results: TurboRAG achieved up to 9.4x reduction in time-to-first-token (TTFT) compared to conventional RAG systems, with an average reduction of 8.6x on LongBench.
After fine-tuning, TurboRAG maintained accuracy within 1% of standard RAG systems on the RGB Benchmark.
Online computational resource utilization was reduced by 98.46% compared to standard RAG, significantly increasing the maximum supported batch size.
solution: TurboRAG transforms the online computation of key-value (KV) caches for retrieved documents into an offline process.
During an offline phase, document chunks are processed by an LLM to generate and store their KV caches alongside their embeddings.
During online inference, relevant precomputed KV caches are retrieved and directly concatenated with the query's KV cache, eliminating the need to re-process documents from scratch.
paper_summary.summary: TurboRAG, developed by Moore Threads AI, significantly reduces inference latency in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems by precomputing and storing key-value (KV) caches for document chunks offline. This approach achieves up to 9.4x faster time-to-first-token while maintaining comparable accuracy to conventional RAG systems by addressing attention and position ID inconsistencies.

title: TurboRAG: Accelerating Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Precomputed KV Caches for Chunked Text

abstract: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) present a promising alternative to dominant autoregressive models (ARMs) by the ability of parallel decoding at the expense of substantial computation and memory costs. Specifically, the cache mechanism for bidirectional attention in dLLMs demands large memory footprint, restricting their ability to handle long contexts under resource-limited settings. Existing cache eviction strategies are designed for ARMs and ignore the unique characteristics of dLLMs, thus leading to unsatisfactory performance. To address these challenges, we introduce MaskKV, a training-free cache eviction framework tailored to dLLMs, focusing on the effect of mask tokens in dLLMs. MaskKV is built on two key innovations: (1) a mask-query guided scoring mechanism that leverages attention weights to identify and evict less critical prompt tokens for each head; (2) an adaptive cache budgeting strategy that improves efficiency by reducing allocation in intermediate layers and concentrating resources on prompt-preferring heads. On LLaDA with MaskKV, compressing the KV cache to only 256 pairs (less than 5% of tokens) retains 94% of the full-cache performance on LongBench and achieves up to 31x acceleration at 32k prompt length. The code is publicly available at: this https URL

organization_info.name: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Mask queries in dLLMs consistently focus on a small set of pivotal prompt tokens across denoising steps, providing a stable, direct signal for identifying critical prompt information.

dLLM layers exhibit a "bimodal importance profile," where the first and last layers show higher representational transformation than middle layers, enabling an offline layer-wise cache allocation strategy.

Attention heads within dLLMs demonstrate functional heterogeneity, with some "information heads" strongly relying on prompts for long-range retrieval and others on mask tokens for discourse planning, informing head-specific budget redistribution.
originalProblem: Autoregressive Models (ARMs) suffer from sequential inference, while dLLMs, though offering parallel decoding, incur high computational and memory costs for long contexts.

dLLMs' bidirectional attention necessitates recomputing and caching Key-Value (KV) features for the entire sequence (prompt, generated, and masked tokens) at every denoising step, escalating storage and update costs.
Existing cache eviction strategies designed for ARMs' causal attention are largely ineffective for dLLMs, limiting their practical deployment for tasks requiring extensive contextual understanding.
results: MaskKV consistently outperforms prior cache eviction strategies on the LongBench benchmark, achieving a 7.02-point improvement over the best baseline under a tight budget of 32 KV pairs for LLaDA-8B.
The framework enables up to 31x faster decoding speed and a 65% lower peak memory footprint for 32K-token prompts, allowing dLLMs to support 8x longer prompts on a consumer-grade RTX 4090 GPU.

MaskKV maintains high generation quality, retaining 94.33% to 98.66% of the dLLM-Cache baseline's performance with a KV budget of only 256 pairs (less than 5% of tokens for 32k context), demonstrating robustness across varied cache sizes.
solution: MaskKV introduces a training-free, two-stage cache eviction framework for dLLMs, grounded in empirical observations of dLLM attention behaviors.
It employs "Mask-Voting" to rank the importance of prompt tokens by aggregating attention scores from mask queries, leveraging their role in identifying critical prompt information.

A hierarchical, budgeted eviction strategy allocates KV cache capacity across layers based on pre-computed importance profiles and redistributes within layers to attention heads based on their prompt preference.
paper_summary.summary: MaskKV, a training-free cache eviction framework, significantly enhances the efficiency of Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) for long-context inference by leveraging mask tokens as "prophets" to identify crucial prompt tokens and implementing a hierarchical budget allocation. This approach achieves up to 31x faster decoding and a 65% lower peak memory footprint, enabling dLLMs to process 8x longer prompts while maintaining generation quality.

title: Mask Tokens as Prophet: Fine-Grained Cache Eviction for Efficient dLLM Inference

abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for long-content
generation (e.g., long Chain-of-Thought reasoning) where decoding efficiency
becomes a critical bottleneck: Autoregressive decoding is inherently limited by
its sequential token generation process, where each token must be generated
before the next can be processed. This sequential dependency restricts the
ability to fully leverage modern hardware's parallel processing capabilities.
Existing methods like speculative decoding and layer skipping offer potential
speedups but have notable drawbacks: speculative decoding relies on an
auxiliary "drafter" model, which can be challenging to acquire and increases
memory overhead, while layer skipping may introduce discrepancies in the
outputs due to the missing key-value cache at skipped layers. In this work, we
propose AdaDecode, which accelerates LLM decoding without requiring auxiliary
models or changes to the original model parameters, while ensuring output
consistency. AdaDecode leverages the insight that many tokens can accurately be
generated at intermediate layers, as further layers often do not significantly
alter predictions once the model reaches a certain confidence. By adaptively
generating tokens at intermediate layers when confidence is high, AdaDecode
enables the next token's computation to begin immediately. The remaining layer
computations for early-predicted tokens are deferred and executed in parallel
with subsequent tokens when needed, maximizing hardware utilization and
reducing decoding latency. A final verification step ensures that early
predictions match the results of standard autoregressive decoding, preserving
output parity. Experiments across diverse generation tasks shows that AdaDecode
consistently achieves superior decoding throughput with up to 1.73x speedup,
while guaranteeing output parity with standard autoregressive decoding.

keyInsights: Many tokens can be accurately predicted at intermediate layers with high confidence, eliminating need for full forward passes
Decomposing LM head weights as $E^* T^{(i)}$ achieves effective early prediction with minimal parameter overhead (48M vs 0.5B+ parameters)
Parallel processing of deferred KV cache computations maximizes hardware utilization without sacrificing output fidelity
originalProblem: Large Language Models face severe decoding bottlenecks due to sequential autoregressive generation, especially as models grow larger and applications demand longer outputs

Existing acceleration methods either require auxiliary drafter models (speculative decoding) with significant memory overhead, or compromise output quality through layer skipping that creates KV cache inconsistencies
Hardware parallelization capabilities remain underutilized by the inherently sequential nature of token-by-token generation
results: Achieves up to 1.73x speedup over standard autoregressive decoding across diverse tasks while maintaining nearly 100% output parity
Demonstrates consistent performance across model sizes from 8B to 34B parameters with low rejection rates (~6% at 0.85 threshold)
Outperforms speculative decoding and other baselines without requiring auxiliary models or architectural modifications
Shows robustness to hyperparameter selection and maintains effectiveness across text summarization, code generation, and mathematical reasoning domains
solution: Introduce trainable lightweight LM heads at intermediate layers that predict tokens early when confidence exceeds threshold γ
Implement adaptive layer parallelism where early predictions trigger immediate processing of next tokens while deferring remaining computations for parallel execution
Deploy rejection sampling verification to guarantee output consistency with standard decoding while maintaining KV cache integrity
paper_summary.summary: University of Virginia researchers develop AdaDecode, an adaptive layer parallelism framework that accelerates LLM decoding by training lightweight language model heads at intermediate layers to make early token predictions with confidence thresholds, then parallelizing remaining computations while maintaining KV cache consistency through deferred processing and rejection sampling verification, achieving up to 1.73x speedup over standard autoregressive decoding across text summarization (XSum), code generation (HumanEval), and mathematical reasoning (GSM8K) tasks while guaranteeing nearly 100% output parity with vanilla decoding, with the method requiring only 48M additional parameters (vs. 0.5B+ for full drafter models) and demonstrating low rejection rates of ~6% at 0.85 confidence threshold, enabling plug-and-play acceleration of existing LLMs without architectural modifications or auxiliary models through a novel decomposed weight matrix design ($E^* T^{(i)}$) that minimizes parameter overhead while maximizing hardware utilization.

title: AdaDecode: Accelerating LLM Decoding with Adaptive Layer Parallelism

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit latent multi-token prediction (MTP) capabilities despite being trained solely for next-token generation. We propose a simple, training-free MTP approach that probes an LLM using on-the-fly mask tokens drawn from its embedding space, enabling parallel prediction of future tokens without modifying model weights or relying on auxiliary draft models. Our method constructs a speculative token tree by sampling top-K candidates from mask-token logits and applies a lightweight pruning strategy to retain high-probability continuations. During decoding, candidate predictions are verified in parallel, resulting in lossless generation while substantially reducing the number of model calls and improving token throughput. Across benchmarks, our probing-based MTP consistently outperforms existing training-free baselines, increasing acceptance length by approximately 12\% on LLaMA3 and 8--12\% on Qwen3, and achieving throughput gains of up to 15--19\%. Finally, we provide theoretical insights and empirical evidence showing that decoder layers naturally align mask-token representations with next-token states, enabling accurate multi-step prediction without retraining or auxiliary models.
title: Efficient Training-Free Multi-Token Prediction via Embedding-Space Probing

abstract: Deployment of autoregressive large language models (LLMs) is costly, and as these models increase in size, the associated costs will become even more considerable. Consequently, different methods have been proposed to accelerate the token generation process and reduce costs. Speculative decoding (SD) is among the most promising approaches to speed up the LLM decoding process by verifying multiple tokens in parallel and using an auxiliary smaller draft model to generate the possible tokens. In SD, usually, one draft model is used to serve a specific target model; however, in practice, LLMs are diverse, and we might need to deal with many target models or more than one target model simultaneously. In this scenario, it is not clear which draft model should be used for which target model, and searching among different draft models or training customized draft models can further increase deployment costs. In this paper, we first introduce a novel multi-target scenario for the deployment of draft models for faster inference. Then, we present a novel, more efficient sorted speculative decoding mechanism that outperforms regular baselines in multi-target settings. We evaluated our method on Spec-Bench in different settings, including base models such as Vicuna 7B, 13B, and LLama Chat 70B. Our results suggest that our draft models perform better than baselines for multiple target models at the same time.
title: S2D: Sorted Speculative Decoding For More Efficient Deployment of Nested Large Language Models

abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates language model inference by separating generation into fast drafting and parallel verification. Its main limitation is drafter-verifier misalignment, which limits token acceptance and reduces overall effectiveness. While small drafting heads trained from scratch compensate with speed, they struggle when verification dominates latency or when inputs are out of distribution. In contrast, pretrained drafters, though slower, achieve higher acceptance rates thanks to stronger standalone generation capabilities, making them competitive when drafting latency is negligible relative to verification or communication overhead. In this work, we aim to improve the acceptance rates of pretrained drafters by introducing a lightweight dynamic alignment mechanism: a steering vector computed from the verifier's hidden states and injected into the pretrained drafter. Compared to existing offline alignment methods such as distillation, our approach boosts the number of accepted tokens by up to 35\% under standard sampling and 22\% under greedy sampling, all while incurring negligible computational overhead. Importantly, our approach can be retrofitted to existing architectures and pretrained models, enabling rapid adoption.
title: Steering Pretrained Drafters during Speculative Decoding

abstract: As large language models (LLMs) scale up, accuracy improves, but the autoregressive (AR) nature of decoding increases latency since each token requires a serial forward pass. Speculative decoding addresses this by employing a fast drafter to propose multi-token drafts, which are then verified in parallel by the target model. However, many deployments still rely on AR drafters, where sequential passes limit wall-clock gains. We revisit the drafting stage and present DiffuSpec, a training-free drop-in framework that uses a pretrained diffusion language model (DLM) to produce multi-token drafts in a single forward pass, while remaining compatible with standard AR verifiers. Because DLM drafts are generated under bidirectional conditioning, parallel per-position candidates form a token lattice in which the locally highest-probability token at each position need not form a causal left-to-right path. Moreover, DLM drafting requires pre-specifying a draft length, inducing a speed-quality trade-off. To address these challenges, we introduce two practical components: (i) a causal-consistency path search (CPS) over this lattice that extracts a left-to-right path aligned with AR verification; and (ii) an adaptive draft-length (ADL) controller that adjusts next proposal size based on recent acceptance feedback and realized generated length. Across benchmarks, DiffuSpec yields up to 3x wall-clock speedup, establishing diffusion-based drafting as a robust alternative to autoregressive drafters for speculative decoding.

keyInsights: Pre-trained Diffusion Language Models, despite their bidirectional nature, can be effectively leveraged as powerful, non-autoregressive drafters for speculative decoding when their outputs are causally aligned.

A systematic path search (CPS) through the DLM's generated token lattice, guided by a causal proxy and intelligent pruning, is crucial for converting bidirectionally generated proposals into AR-compatible sequences with high acceptance rates.

Dynamically adapting the draft length (ADL) based on online feedback is essential for translating higher token acceptance into actual wall-clock speedup, preventing inefficiencies from over- or under-drafting.
originalProblem: Autoregressive (AR) decoding in Large Language Models (LLMs) suffers from inherent latency due to token-by-token generation, hindering real-time applications.
Existing speculative decoding methods often rely on sequential AR drafters, which limit wall-clock speedup, or incur substantial training and maintenance costs for custom drafters.

Integrating bidirectionally-conditioned Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) as drafters presents challenges in aligning their output with the strict left-to-right causal requirements of AR verifiers and managing fixed, non-optimal draft lengths.
results: DiffuSpec achieved a mean speedup of 3.08x and a Mean Accepted Tokens (MAT) of 6.99 across six diverse tasks on the Spec-Bench protocol, establishing the leading performance among all training-free baselines.

It delivered a 1.41x speedup and an increase of 0.81 MAT compared to traditional Speculative Parallel Sampling (SPS) which uses an AR drafter, demonstrating the efficiency gains from non-autoregressive drafting.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical and complementary roles of both Causal-Consistency Path Search (CPS) and Adaptive Draft-Length (ADL), with CPS primarily improving MAT by +0.90 and ADL optimizing speedup by dynamically managing draft proposals.
solution: DiffuSpec employs pre-trained Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) as training-free, high-throughput drafters for speculative decoding, leveraging their ability to propose multi-token blocks in a single forward pass.

It introduces Causal-Consistency Path Search (CPS) to extract a causally consistent path from the DLM's bidirectional token lattice, using entropy-adaptive pruning and beam search to maximize acceptance by the autoregressive verifier.
An Adaptive Draft-Length (ADL) controller dynamically adjusts the proposed draft length based on real-time feedback (generated and accepted tokens) to optimize the speed-quality trade-off.
paper_summary.summary: DiffuSpec, developed by researchers from Tsinghua University, Southern University of Science and Technology, and OPPO Research Institute, introduces a training-free framework that utilizes pre-trained Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) as high-throughput drafters for speculative decoding. This approach significantly accelerates Large Language Model inference by up to 3.08x while preserving output quality, addressing latency bottlenecks without requiring additional model training.

title: DiffuSpec: Unlocking Diffusion Language Models for Speculative Decoding

abstract: Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) enable parallel token decoding, providing a promising alternative to the sequential nature of autoregressive generation. However, their iterative denoising process remains computationally expensive because it repeatedly processes the entire sequence at every step. We observe that across these diffusion steps, most token representations remain stable; only a small subset, which we term salient tokens, contributes meaningfully to the next update. Leveraging this temporal sparsity, we present DyLLM, a training-free inference framework that accelerates decoding by selectively computing only these salient tokens. DyLLM identifies saliency by measuring the cosine similarity of attention contexts between adjacent denoising steps. It recomputes feed-forward and attention operations only for salient tokens while reusing cached activations for the remainder. Across diverse reasoning and code-generation benchmarks, DyLLM achieves up to 9.6x higher throughput while largely preserving the baseline accuracy of state-of-the-art models like LLaDA and Dream.

keyInsights: A majority of token representations remain largely stable across consecutive denoising steps in MDLMs, indicating significant "temporal sparsity" that can be exploited for computational efficiency.
Token representation stability exhibits layer-wise variation, with initial layers showing higher stability compared to deeper layers, suggesting the need for layer-adaptive computation.
The L2 error in a Transformer block's normalized output is bounded by the temporal cosine similarity of its input attention contexts, theoretically validating saliency-based FFN skipping.
originalProblem: Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) face high computational costs during inference due to the iterative denoising process requiring repeated full sequence reprocessing.
This iterative nature leads to substantial overhead, primarily from Feed-Forward Network (FFN) computations, despite their parallel token generation capability.
Prior caching strategies often use fixed schedules or global thresholds, which do not adapt to the dynamic, layer-wise variations in token representation stability inherent to diffusion.
results: DyLLM achieved up to 7.6x higher throughput for LLaDA 8B Instruct and up to 9.6x for Dream 7B Instruct compared to baselines, without requiring model retraining.
The framework preserved near-lossless accuracy across various benchmarks, with some instances showing slight improvements, attributed to dynamic, layer-adaptive computation.

DyLLM exhibited robust scalability with increasing degrees of parallel decoding (nu), effectively overcoming the performance bottlenecks of periodic full sequence refresh steps found in previous methods.
solution: A layer-adaptive saliency mechanism dynamically identifies semantically changing tokens by measuring temporal cosine similarity of attention contexts across denoising steps, enabling selective FFN computation.
A saliency-aware approximate attention mechanism performs full attention updates for salient tokens and approximates updates for non-salient tokens by leveraging only the changes in value vectors.

Response-only steps periodically restrict input processing to only the response tokens, focusing computation on regions of higher semantic change, while maintaining occasional full sequence context updates.
paper_summary.summary: DyLLM presents a training-free inference framework for Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) that leverages layer-adaptive saliency and approximate attention to identify and selectively recompute only semantically changing tokens. This method achieves up to 7.6x higher throughput for LLaDA 8B and 9.6x for Dream 7B, maintaining accuracy and exhibiting robust scalability with parallel decoding.

title: DyLLM: Efficient Diffusion LLM Inference via Saliency-based Token Selection and Partial Attention

abstract: Large language models typically generate tokens autoregressively, using each token as input for the next. Recent work on Speculative Decoding has sought to accelerate this process by employing a smaller, faster draft model to more quickly generate candidate tokens. These candidates are then verified in parallel by the larger (original) verify model, resulting in overall speedup compared to using the larger model by itself in an autoregressive fashion. In this work, we introduce AMUSD (Asynchronous Multi-device Speculative Decoding), a system that further accelerates generation by decoupling the draft and verify phases into a continuous, asynchronous approach. Unlike conventional speculative decoding, where only one model (draft or verify) performs token generation at a time, AMUSD enables both models to perform predictions independently on separate devices (e.g., GPUs). We evaluate our approach over multiple datasets and show that AMUSD achieves an average 29% improvement over speculative decoding and up to 1.96$\times$ speedup over conventional autoregressive decoding, while achieving identical output quality. Our system is open-source and available at this https URL.
title: AMUSD: Asynchronous Multi-Device Speculative Decoding for LLM Acceleration

abstract: Speculative decoding (SD) is a powerful technique for accelerating the inference process of large language models (LLMs) without sacrificing accuracy. Typically, SD employs a small draft model to generate a fixed number of draft tokens, which are then verified in parallel by the target model. However, our experiments reveal that the optimal draft length varies significantly across different decoding steps. This variation suggests that using a fixed draft length limits the potential for further improvements in decoding speed. To address this challenge, we propose Pacer, a novel approach that dynamically controls draft length using a lightweight, trainable pre-verification layer. This layer pre-verifies draft tokens blockwise before they are sent to the target model, allowing the draft model to stop token generation if the blockwise pre-verification fails. We implement Pacer on multiple SD model pairs and evaluate its performance across various benchmarks. Our results demonstrate that Pacer achieves up to 2.66x Speedup over autoregressive decoding and consistently outperforms standard speculative decoding. Furthermore, when integrated with Ouroboros, Pacer attains up to 3.09x Speedup.

keyInsights: The optimal number of tokens that can be speculated and accepted varies significantly across different decoding steps, demonstrating the inefficiency of fixed draft lengths.
Acceptance rates of draft tokens sharply decline with increasing draft position, highlighting the importance of positional information for predicting acceptance likelihood.

Leveraging a lightweight, trainable pre-verification layer with contextual and positional information provides a more robust and accurate method for dynamic draft length control than heuristic-based approaches.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from significant inference latency due to their autoregressive, token-by-token generation process, limiting real-time applications and scalability.

Existing speculative decoding (SD) methods use fixed draft lengths, which are inefficient; small lengths terminate early, wasting target model calls, while large lengths generate many rejected tokens, wasting draft model computation.
Prior dynamic draft length methods rely on unreliable heuristics or resource-intensive parallelization, struggling with LLM miscalibration and competition for GPU resources.
results: Achieved consistent and substantial speedups, up to 2.66x over vanilla autoregressive decoding and outperforming standard speculative decoding (SD) and other baselines on HumanEval and SpecBench.
Outperformed state-of-the-art dynamic draft length methods like AdaEDL and SpecDec++ in decoding speed and average acceptance length across various LLMs and datasets.

Demonstrated orthogonal benefits by integrating with Ouroboros, yielding further performance gains up to 3.09x speedup, and confirmed the crucial role of positional embeddings and an adaptive threshold growth factor through ablation studies.
solution: PACER introduces a trainable blockwise pre-verification layer (a single-layer Transformer) that generates draft tokens in fixed-size blocks and evaluates their acceptance likelihood.
The pre-verification layer incorporates hidden states of draft tokens, positional embeddings within the speculation window, and previously accepted token contexts to make informed decisions.
Drafting halts dynamically based on the mean estimated acceptance rate of a block against an adaptive threshold, after which accumulated drafts are sent for parallel verification by the target model.
paper_summary.summary: PACER introduces a trainable blockwise pre-verification layer for speculative decoding, enabling dynamic adjustment of draft lengths to accelerate Large Language Model inference. It achieves up to 2.66x speedup over autoregressive decoding, and 3.09x when combined with Ouroboros, while preserving output quality across various LLMs and tasks.

title: PACER: Blockwise Pre-verification for Speculative Decoding with Adaptive Length

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) commonly employ autoregressive generation during inference, leading to high memory bandwidth demand and consequently extended latency. To mitigate this inefficiency, we present Bi-directional Tuning for lossless Acceleration (BiTA), an innovative method expediting LLMs via streamlined semi-autoregressive generation and draft verification. Inspired by the concept of prompt tuning, we enhance LLMs with a parameter-efficient design called bi-directional tuning for the capability in semi-autoregressive generation. Employing efficient tree-based decoding, the models perform draft candidate generation and verification in parallel, ensuring outputs identical to their autoregressive counterparts under greedy sampling. BiTA serves as a lightweight plug-in module, seamlessly boosting the inference efficiency of existing LLMs without requiring additional assistance models or incurring significant extra memory costs. Applying the proposed BiTA, LLaMA-2-70B-Chat achieves a 2.7$\times$ speedup on the MT-Bench benchmark. Extensive experiments confirm our method surpasses state-of-the-art acceleration techniques.

keyInsights: Parameter-efficient tuning, using a very small number of soft embeddings (prompt and mask tokens), can effectively instill SAR generation capabilities into pre-trained, frozen LLMs.

Integrating generation and verification into a single forward pass via a sophisticated tree-based attention mechanism is crucial for minimizing memory bandwidth bottlenecks and achieving streamlined, lossless acceleration.

Bi-directional tuning, by applying soft prompts in every transformer block and using both prefix and suffix embeddings, enables the model to capture richer contextual features for more robust future token predictions.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from high inference latency due to their autoregressive (token-by-token) generation, requiring numerous sequential transformer calls.

Existing semi-autoregressive (SAR) methods often degrade output quality or require extensive retraining, while traditional speculative decoding incurs overhead from external draft models or multi-step verification.
The memory-bandwidth cost, rather than computational cost, bottlenecks LLM inference, making them less suitable for real-time and resource-limited applications.
results: BiTA achieves significant speedups ranging from 2.1x to 3.3x across various LLMs (LLaMA-2-7B/70B, Vicuna-7B/33B, Falcon-40B) on diverse tasks, including a 2.7x speedup for LLaMA-2-70B-Chat on MT-Bench.
The method introduces minimal additional trainable parameters, constituting approximately 0.06% of total model parameters for 7B-scale models and even less for larger models.

BiTA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art speculative decoding methods, including Medusa by 19-32% and other self-speculative approaches like Self-SpecDec and Lookahead, in terms of speedup without compromising output quality.
solution: Bi-directional tuning introduces a minimal set of learnable 'prompt tokens' and 'mask tokens' as soft embeddings to adapt frozen LLMs for semi-autoregressive (SAR) draft generation.
A specialized attention mask is used to enable prompt tokens to influence mask tokens' predictions without altering the original input's attention patterns.

An efficient tree-based decoding strategy is employed, allowing simultaneous AR generation, SAR generation of multiple draft candidates, and draft verification within a single forward pass using tree-based attention.
paper_summary.summary: BiTA, a method developed by Intellifusion and Harbin Institute of Technology, achieves lossless acceleration for large language models by integrating bi-directional tuning with an efficient tree-based decoding strategy. It demonstrates speedups ranging from 2.1x to 3.3x across various LLMs and tasks, ensuring generated output quality remains identical to standard autoregressive decoding.

title: BiTA: Bi-Directional Tuning for Lossless Acceleration in Large Language Models

abstract: In autoregressive language models, each token is sampled by conditioning on all the past tokens; the overall string has thus been sampled from the correct underlying joint distribution represented by the model. In contrast, masked diffusion language models generate text by unmasking tokens out of order and potentially in parallel. Generating an overall string sampled from the correct underlying joint distribution would (again) require exactly one token unmasking in every full-model forward pass. The more tokens unmasked in parallel, the further away the string is from the true joint; this can be seen in the resulting drop in accuracy (but, increase in speed). In this paper we devise a way to {\em approximately} sample multiple tokens from the joint distribution in a single full-model forward pass; we do so by developing a new lightweight single-layer ``sampler" on top of an existing large diffusion LM. One forward pass of the full model can now be followed by multiple forward passes of only this sampler layer, to yield multiple unmasked tokens. Our sampler is trained to mimic exact joint sampling from the (frozen) full model. We show the effectiveness of our approximate joint sampling for both pretrained-only (Dream-7B-Base, Llada-7B-Base) and instruction-tuned (Dream-7B-Instruct, Dream-7B-Coder) models on language modeling and math \& coding tasks. When four tokens are unmasked for each full-model denoising step, our sampling algorithm achieves a MAUVE score of 0.87 (vs marginal baseline of 0.31) with respect to the true joint distribution.
title: Enabling Approximate Joint Sampling in Diffusion LMs

abstract:
Speculative decoding is a prominent technique to speed up the inference of a
large target language model based on predictions of an auxiliary draft model.
While effective, in application-specific settings, it often involves
fine-tuning both draft and target models to achieve high acceptance rates. As
the number of downstream tasks grows, these draft models add significant
complexity to inference systems. We propose Speculative Streaming, a
single-model speculative decoding method that fuses drafting into the target
model by changing the fine-tuning objective from next token prediction to
future n-gram prediction. Speculative Streaming speeds up decoding by 1.8 -
3.1X in a diverse set of tasks, such as Summarization, Structured Queries, and
Meaning Representation, without sacrificing generation quality. Additionally,
Speculative Streaming is parameter-efficient. It achieves on-par/higher
speed-ups than Medusa-style architectures while using ~10000X fewer extra
parameters, making it well-suited for resource-constrained devices.

keyInsights: A single large language model can be modified to perform both speculative drafting and verification simultaneously, eliminating the need for separate draft models.
Replacing standard multi-head attention (MHA) with Multi-Stream Attention (MSA) in the upper layers of a transformer model enables parallel processing of main and speculative token streams.
Implementing tree-based drafting and pruning allows the model to efficiently explore and accept multiple future tokens within a single forward pass, optimized for resource management.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) suffer from high computational demands and inference latency, hindering their deployment, especially on resource-constrained devices.
Traditional speculative decoding methods, while accelerating LLM inference, require maintaining separate 'draft' and 'target' models, leading to increased memory footprint and system complexity.
Existing single-model speculative decoding attempts often add substantial auxiliary parameters, reducing their efficiency benefits.
results: Achieved speedups ranging from 1.8x to 3.1x compared to standard autoregressive decoding across various LLM sizes (7B, 13B, 70B).
Maintained generation quality on par with LoRA fine-tuned base models, demonstrating no degradation in output fidelity.
Required approximately 10,000 times fewer additional parameters compared to Medusa-style architectures, significantly enhancing parameter and memory efficiency.
solution: The Speculative Streaming approach modifies a single target LLM by replacing standard multi-head attention layers with Multi-Stream Attention (MSA) layers in its upper portion.
Stream embeddings are introduced to differentiate between the main token stream and multiple speculative streams, allowing the unified model to process them in parallel.
A tree drafting and pruning mechanism enables the model to sample and verify multiple candidate token paths efficiently within the same forward pass.
paper_summary.summary: Speculative Streaming proposes a method for accelerating large language model inference by enabling speculative decoding within a single model architecture. This approach eliminates the need for separate draft models, achieving 1.8x to 3.1x speedups while maintaining generation quality and significantly reducing parameter overhead compared to prior methods.

title: Speculative Streaming: Fast LLM Inference without Auxiliary Models

abstract:
Traditional language models operate autoregressively, i.e., they predict one
token at a time. Rapid explosion in model sizes has resulted in high inference
times. In this work, we propose DynaMo, a suite of multi-token prediction
language models that reduce net inference times. Our models
$\textit{dynamically}$ predict multiple tokens based on their confidence in the
predicted joint probability distribution. We propose a lightweight technique to
train these models, leveraging the weights of traditional autoregressive
counterparts. Moreover, we propose novel ways to enhance the estimated joint
probability to improve text generation quality, namely co-occurrence weighted
masking and adaptive thresholding. We also propose systematic qualitative and
quantitative methods to rigorously test the quality of generated text for
non-autoregressive generation. One of the models in our suite, DynaMo-7.3B-T3,
achieves same-quality generated text as the baseline (Pythia-6.9B) while
achieving 2.57$\times$ speed-up with only 5.87% and 2.67% parameter and
training time overheads, respectively.
title:
DynaMo: Accelerating Language Model Inference with Dynamic Multi-Token
  Sampling

abstract: The massive adoption of large language models (LLMs) demands efficient deployment strategies. However, the auto-regressive decoding process, which is fundamental to how most LLMs generate text, poses challenges to achieve efficient serving. In this work, we introduce a parallel auto-regressive generation method. By instruct-tuning on general domain data that contains hierarchical structures, we enable LLMs to independently plan their generation process and perform auto-parallel auto-regressive (APAR) generation, significantly reducing the number of generation steps. APAR alone can achieve up to 2x speed-up, and when combined with speculative decoding, the speed-up can reach up to 4x. In addition, APAR reduces the key-value cache consumption and attention computation during generation. This leads to a throughput increase of 20-70% and a latency reduce of 20-35% in high-throughput scenarios, compared to state-of-the-art serving frameworks.

keyInsights: Large Language Models can be trained to autonomously recognize and exploit internal parallelizable structures within text, allowing them to initiate and manage parallel decoding threads.

Integrating parallelism directly into the decoding process through hierarchical structures and specialized control tokens can significantly reduce sequential steps, attention computation, and KV cache memory footprint.

This intrinsic parallelization method is complementary to existing inference acceleration techniques (like speculative decoding and paged attention), leading to synergistic performance gains without compromising generation quality.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from sub-optimal generation speed due to their inherently sequential, token-by-token auto-regressive decoding process.
High attention computation costs, scaling linearly with sequence length, and extensive Key-Value (KV) cache consumption limit serving throughput and the number of concurrent requests.
Existing inference acceleration methods primarily rely on external mechanisms or re-training, lacking intrinsic parallelism within the LLM's decoding process itself.
results: APAR-enabled models achieved an average generation speed increase of 2x on Vicuna Bench and 1.4x on MT Bench, further accelerating to 4x and 2.9x respectively when combined with speculative decoding.

In high-throughput scenarios, APAR improved serving throughput by 20% to 70% and reduced average end-to-end decoding latency by 20% to 35% compared to original models, demonstrating comparable latency to models half its size.

The method reduced maximum KV cache slots required by 12% to 27% and decreased the average number of attended tokens per prediction by 15% to 35%, while maintaining generation quality with negligible difference (within ±2%) on evaluation benchmarks.
solution: APAR fine-tunes LLMs to identify and exploit hierarchical text structures (e.g., lists, paragraphs) using a 'paragraph tree' representation for responses.
It introduces two control tokens, `[Fork]` for initiating parallel threads and `[Child]` for guiding sub-paragraph generation, managed by a modified decoding algorithm.

The decoding process enables concurrent generation, early release of KV cache from completed child sequences, and a tree-based attention mechanism where tokens only attend to their ancestors, reducing computation.
paper_summary.summary: APAR, developed by researchers from Tsinghua University and Zhipu AI, introduces an auto-parallel auto-regressive decoding method that enables large language models to autonomously generate text concurrently by recognizing and leveraging hierarchical structures. This approach achieves up to 4x faster generation in memory-bound scenarios and improves serving throughput by 20-70% in high-throughput settings, all while preserving output quality.

title: APAR: LLMs Can Do Auto-Parallel Auto-Regressive Decoding

abstract: The autoregressive nature of large language models (LLMs) fundamentally limits inference speed, as each forward pass generates only a single token and is often bottlenecked by memory bandwidth. Speculative decoding has emerged as a promising solution, adopting a draft-then-verify strategy to accelerate token generation. While the EAGLE series achieves strong acceleration, its requirement of training a separate draft head for each target model introduces substantial adaptation costs. In this work, we propose \textbf{PARD (PARallel Draft)}, a novel speculative decoding method featuring \textit{target-independence} and \textit{parallel token prediction}. Specifically, PARD enables a single draft model to be applied across an entire family of target models without requiring separate training for each variant, thereby minimizing adaptation costs. Meanwhile, PARD substantially accelerates inference by predicting multiple future tokens within a single forward pass of the draft phase. To further reduce the training adaptation cost of PARD, we propose a COnditional Drop-token (COD) mechanism based on the integrity of prefix key-value states, enabling autoregressive draft models to be adapted into parallel draft models at low-cost. Our experiments show that the proposed COD method improves draft model training efficiency by \textbf{3$\times$} compared with traditional masked prediction training. On the \texttt{vLLM} inference framework, PARD achieves up to \textbf{3.67$\times$} speedup on LLaMA3.1-8B, reaching \textbf{264.88} tokens per second, which is \textbf{1.15$\times$} faster than EAGLE-3. Our code is available at this https URL.
title: PARD: Accelerating LLM Inference with Low-Cost PARallel Draft Model Adaptation

abstract: Large language models such as GPT and Llama are trained with a next-token prediction loss. In this work, we suggest that training language models to predict multiple future tokens at once results in higher sample efficiency. More specifically, at each position in the training corpus, we ask the model to predict the following n tokens using n independent output heads, operating on top of a shared model trunk. Considering multi-token prediction as an auxiliary training task, we measure improved downstream capabilities with no overhead in training time for both code and natural language models. The method is increasingly useful for larger model sizes and keeps its appeal when training for multiple epochs. Gains are especially pronounced on generative benchmarks like coding, where our models consistently outperform strong baselines by several percentage points. Our 13B parameter models solves 12 % more problems on HumanEval and 17 % more on MBPP than comparable next-token models. Experiments on small algorithmic tasks demonstrate that multi-token prediction is favorable for the development of induction heads and algorithmic reasoning capabilities. As an additional benefit, models trained with 4-token prediction are up to 3X times faster at inference, even with large batch sizes.
title: Distribution-Aware Companding Quantization of Large Language Models

abstract: Scaling the size of language models to tens of billions of parameters has led to impressive performance on a wide range of tasks.
